pages.title: 1. Construction of Cartan subgroups and maximal tori for a smooth algebraic group

blocks.html: 1. Construction of Cartan subgroups and maximal tori for a smooth algebraic group

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\). Then \(G\) admits a maximal torus \(T\), hence a Cartan subgroup \(C = \mathrm{Centr}_G(T)\).

By virtue of XII 3.2, it amounts to the same thing to find a maximal torus \(T\) of \(G\), or a Cartan subgroup \(C\) of \(G\). Moreover, since the maximal tori of \(G\) are those of \(G^0\), we may assume \(G\) is connected. We distinguish two cases:

1°) The field \(k\) is finite. Let \(\mathscr{T}\) be the scheme of maximal tori of \(G\) (Exp. XII 1.10), which is a smooth scheme over \(k\). Note that \(G\) acts on \(\mathscr{T}\) via inner automorphisms, and by virtue of the conjugation theorem (XII 6.6 a)), two points of \(\mathscr{T}_{\overline{k}}\) rational over \(\overline{k}\) are congruent under \(G_{\overline{k}}(\overline{k})\). Taking into account that \(\mathscr{T}\) is smooth over \(k\), hence \(\mathscr{T}_{\overline{k}}\) smooth over \(\overline{k}\), it follows that \(\mathscr{T}_{\overline{k}}\) is isomorphic to \(G_{\overline{k}}/\mathrm{N}\), where \(\mathrm{N}\) is the stabilizer of an element of \(\mathscr{T}_{\overline{k}}(\overline{k})\), i.e. the normalizer of a maximal torus \(\overline{T}\) of \(G_{\overline{k}}\). Consequently, \(\mathscr{T}\) is a "homogeneous space" under the action of the group \(G\). A well-known theorem of Lang (Amer. J. Math. 78, 1956, pp. 555-563) tells us that every homogeneous space under a smooth connected algebraic group over a finite field \(k\) admits a rational point. In particular, \(\mathscr{T}\) admits a rational point, i.e. \(G\) admits a maximal torus \(T\). Q.E.D.
2°) The field \(k\) is infinite. We will use the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\). Then \(G\) admits a subgroup of type (C) (XIII 6.2).

By virtue of XIII 6.3 it amounts to the same thing to say that \(\mathfrak{g}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\). This is trivial if \(k\) is infinite, for then \(\mathfrak{g}\) contains a regular element \(a\), and we take \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a, \mathfrak{g})\). The case \(k\) finite is treated exactly as in the proof of 1°) above, but requires the prior construction of the scheme \(\mathscr{D}\) of Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) and the fact that the latter is smooth over \(k\), which will be seen below (2.16).
To establish 1.1 in case 2°) where we have placed ourselves, it will suffice in any event to know 1.2 for \(k\) infinite. Let us also note for the record:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3

Let \(G\) be a smooth, connected, affine algebraic group whose reductive center (XII 4.1 and 4.4) is reduced to the unit group. Then \(G\) is nilpotent if (and only if) its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent.
This is contained in XII 4.9.

We can now give a procedure for constructing Cartan subgroups of \(G\) (valid also when \(k\) is finite, granting 1.2 in that case). Suppose first that \(G\) is affine. We proceed by induction on \(n = \dim G\), the assertion being trivial if \(n = 0\). So suppose \(n > 0\) and the assertion proved for dimensions \(n' < n\). Let \(Z\) be the reductive center of \(G\), and let \[ u : G \longrightarrow G' = G/Z \] be the canonical homomorphism. By virtue of XII 4.7 c), there is a one-to-one correspondence \(C' \mapsto C = u^{-1}(C')\) between Cartan subgroups of \(G'\) and Cartan subgroups of \(G\). Hence, replacing \(G\) by \(G'\) if necessary, we may assume that the reductive center \(Z\) of \(G\) is reduced to the identity element (for this is the case for that of \(G'\) by XII 4.7 b)). By virtue of 1.3, \(G\) admits a subgroup \(D\) of type (C). We know that over the algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\), \(D_{\overline{k}}\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(G_{\overline{k}}\) (XIII 6.6 b)), hence every Cartan subgroup of \(D\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(G\) (XIII, 2.8 a)). So we are reduced to finding a Cartan subgroup of \(D\). If \(\dim D = \dim G\), i.e. \(D = G\), then the Lie algebra of \(G\) is a Cartan subalgebra of itself, hence is nilpotent, hence by virtue of 1.3 \(G\) is nilpotent, hence is a Cartan subgroup of itself. If \(\dim D < \dim G\), then by the induction hypothesis there exists a Cartan subgroup of \(D\), which is therefore a Cartan subgroup of \(G\), which completes the proof in the case where \(G\) is affine. In the general case, let \(Z\) be the center of \(G\); then \(G/Z = G'\) is affine (XII 6.1) and for every Cartan subgroup \(C'\) of \(G'\), its inverse image \(C\) in \(G\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(G\) (XII 6.6 e)). We are reduced to finding a Cartan subgroup of the smooth affine algebraic group \(G'\), a case which has already been treated.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

Let \(G\) be a smooth group scheme of finite type over an artinian scheme \(S\). Then \(G\) admits a maximal torus \(T\), hence a Cartan subgroup \(C = \mathrm{Centr}_G(T)\). Every torus \(S\) in \(G\) is contained in a maximal torus.

Indeed, we may assume \(S\) is local with residue field \(k\); then by virtue of 1.1, \(G_0 = G \times_S \mathrm{Spec}(k)\) admits a maximal torus \(T_0\); by virtue of IX 3.6 bis and X 2.3, \(T_0\) comes from a torus \(T\) of \(G\), which is evidently a maximal torus. The last statement follows from this, by applying the preceding result to the centralizer of \(S\), which is indeed smooth over \(k\) (XI 2.4).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 1.5

a) We will give below (3.20, 3.21 and XV) variants of 1.4 in the case where \(S\) is not assumed to be artinian.

b) I do not know whether every algebraic group \(G\) (not necessarily smooth) over a field \(k\) admits a maximal torus. The question arises only in characteristic \(p > 0\), and using 1.1 for a smooth quotient group of the form \(G' = G/I\), where \(I\) is a suitable radicial subgroup of \(G\) (for example, the kernel of a sufficiently high power of the Frobenius homomorphism), and taking the inverse image in \(G\) of a maximal torus of \(G'\), we are reduced to the case where \((G_{\overline{k}})_{\mathrm{red}}\) is a torus (\(\overline{k}\) still denoting the algebraic closure of \(k\)). It is easy to see that the answer is affirmative when \(G\) is commutative (or more generally nilpotent): then \(G\) admits a unique maximal torus, which can be constructed for example by descent from the maximal torus of \(G_{\overline{k}}\)1.

c) In the case where \(G\) is affine, and \(k\) perfect or \(G\) solvable, 1.1 is known and due to Rosenlicht; his proof is very different from the one given here.

d) When \(k\) is infinite, 1.1 is a consequence of the much more precise result that the scheme \(\mathscr{T}\) of maximal tori of \(G\) is a rational variety, proved below (6.1). The method is essentially the combination of the proof of 1.1 and of the explicit description of the structure of the scheme \(\mathscr{D}\) of Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\). To reach the desired result, we must first generalize to the case of an arbitrary base prescheme certain results of XIII 4 to 6 (this is the aim of the next two sections), and refine the preceding construction proving 1.1, using the fact that every Cartan subgroup of \(G\) is contained in one and only one subgroup of type (C) of \(G\) (Sections 4, 5).

footnotes.html: M. Raynaud has given a negative answer to the question raised here, cf. XVII Example 5.9.c).

pages.title: 2. Lie algebras over an arbitrary prescheme: regular sections and Cartan subalgebras

blocks.html: 2. Lie algebras over an arbitrary prescheme: regular sections and Cartan subalgebras

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(\mathfrak{d}\) a Lie algebra over \(A\), and for every \(s \in \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), let \(\mathfrak{d}(s) = \mathfrak{d} \otimes_A k(s)\) be the corresponding Lie algebra over the residue field \(k(s)\). Suppose the \(A\)-module \(\mathfrak{d}\) is of finite presentation. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    For every \(s \in \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathfrak{d}(s)\) is nilpotent.

  2. (ii)

    For every \(x \in \mathfrak{d}\), \(\mathrm{ad}(x)\) is nilpotent.

  3. (iii)

    There exists an integer \(\mathrm{N} \geqslant 0\) such that for every sequence \(x_1, \ldots, x_{\mathrm{N}}\) of elements of \(\mathfrak{d}\), we have \[ \mathrm{ad}(x_1)\,\mathrm{ad}(x_2)\ldots\mathrm{ad}(x_{\mathrm{N}}) = 0. \]

When \(A\) is a field, the equivalence of (i) and (iii) is the definition of "nilpotent", that of (ii) and (iii) is a well-known consequence of Engel's theorem (Bourbaki, Groupes et algèbres de Lie, Chap. I, §4, No2). In the general case, we have trivially (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii), and (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) thanks to the preceding result and to the fact that (ii) is stable under passage to the quotient and under localization. It remains to prove (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii). When \(A\) is artinian local with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), let \(n > 0\) be an integer such that \(\mathfrak{m}^n = 0\), let \(\mathrm{N}\) be an integer such that condition (iii) is satisfied for \(\mathfrak{d}(s) = \mathfrak{d} \otimes_A A/\mathfrak{m}\); taking \(\mathrm{N}' = n\mathrm{N}\), one sees at once that this integer satisfies (iii). When \(A\) is noetherian, then there exist finitely many elements \(s_i \in \mathrm{Spec}(A)\) and ideals of definition \(\mathfrak{q}_i \subset A_{s_i}\), such that the natural map \[ \mathfrak{d} \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathfrak{d} \otimes_A A_i, \quad \text{where } A_i = A_{s_i}/\mathfrak{q}_i, \] is injective; then by virtue of what precedes there exists for every \(i\) an integer \(\mathrm{N}_i\) satisfying (iii) for the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{d} \otimes_A A_i\), and taking for \(\mathrm{N}\) the largest of the \(\mathrm{N}_i\), one satisfies (iii) for \(\mathfrak{d}\). Finally, the general case reduces to the noetherian case by the usual procedure explained in EGA IV 8.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(\mathfrak{d}\) a quasi-coherent Lie algebra over \(S\), which is a module of finite presentation. We say that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is locally nilpotent if for every \(s \in S\), the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{d}(s)\) over \(k(s)\) is nilpotent. We say that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is strictly locally nilpotent if it is locally free, and if on every open set \(U\) of \(S\) on which it is of constant rank \(r\), its Killing polynomial reduces to \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathfrak{d}}(t) = t^r\).

Of course, if \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a Lie algebra which is locally free (as a module) over \(S\), we define its Killing polynomial as a polynomial \[ \mathrm{P}_{\mathfrak{d}} \in A[t], \quad \text{where } A = \Gamma(\mathrm{Sym}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathfrak{d})) \simeq \Gamma(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{d})) \] is the ring of sections of the structure sheaf of the vector bundle \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{d})\) defined by \(\mathfrak{d}\).
It is evident that the two notions just introduced in 2.2 are stable under base change, and of local nature for the fpqc topology. Let us note:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

If \(\mathfrak{d}\) is strictly locally nilpotent, it is locally nilpotent. The converse is true when \(\mathfrak{d}\) is locally free and \(S\) reduced.
The proof is immediate.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.4

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(\mathfrak{g}\) a Lie algebra over \(S\) which is a locally free module of finite type. Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\); we say that it is a Cartan subalgebra if it satisfies the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a submodule which is locally a direct factor (hence also locally free of finite type).

  2. (ii)

    For every \(s \in S\), \(\mathfrak{d}(s)\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.5

Let \(S\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) be as in 2.4. A section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is called quasi-regular if for every \(s \in S\), \(a(s) \in \mathfrak{g}(s)\) is a regular element of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) over \(k(s)\). We say that \(a\) is a regular section if it is quasi-regular and contained in a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

The notions 2.4 and 2.5 are again stable under base change, and of local nature for the fpqc topology. Only the last assertion, and for the case of the notion "regular section", requires a proof, and comes from the fact that the Cartan subalgebra containing a given regular section is uniquely determined. More precisely:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.6

Let \(S\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) be as in 2.4 and let \(a\) be a quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then there exists at most one Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\). For one to exist, i.e. for \(a\) to be a regular section, it is necessary and sufficient that \(a\) satisfy the following condition: \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a submodule of \(\mathfrak{g}\) which is locally a direct factor, and \(\mathrm{ad}(a)\) induces an automorphism of \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}\). In that case, \(\mathfrak{d}\) is the unique Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\).

Suppose indeed that \(a\) is contained in the Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) is bijective in each fibre, hence (since \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}\) is locally free of finite type) it is an automorphism of \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}\); on the other hand by virtue of 2.1 \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{d}}\) is locally nilpotent, hence \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\), which proves the uniqueness of \(\mathfrak{d}\), and the necessity of the announced criterion of regularity. For the sufficiency, note that the hypothesis made on \(a\) implies that the formation of \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) commutes with extension of the base and in particular with passage to the fibres, which shows in particular that the fibres \(\mathfrak{d}(s)\) of \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) are Cartan subalgebras of the \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\); moreover \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) by virtue of XIII 4.1, hence it is a Cartan subalgebra.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7

Under the conditions of 2.4, let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), \(a\) a section of \(\mathfrak{d}\) which is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\), \(u\) an automorphism of \(\mathfrak{g}\). For \(u\) to leave \(\mathfrak{d}\) invariant, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u(a)\) be a section of \(\mathfrak{d}\).

Indeed, by transport of structure \(u(a)\) is then a regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}\), contained in the two Cartan subalgebras \(\mathfrak{d}\) and \(u(\mathfrak{d})\), which are therefore identical.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8

Under the conditions of 2.4, let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then for every \(s \in S\) such that \(k(s)\) is infinite, there exist an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a regular section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) over \(V\), such that \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a, \mathfrak{g}|_V)\) (i.e. such that \(a\) is a section of \(\mathfrak{d}|_V\)).

Indeed, the fact that \(k(s)\) is infinite ensures the existence of a regular element \(a_0\) of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) contained in \(\mathfrak{d}(s)\); one can extend it to a section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{d}\) over an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\), and since \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) induces an automorphism of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)/\mathfrak{d}(s)\), it follows that, upon shrinking \(V\), \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) is an automorphism, which implies that \(a\) is a quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}|_V\), which satisfies the desired condition.

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be as in 2.4; then examination of its Killing polynomial implies at once that the function \[ s \longmapsto \text{nilpotent rank of } \mathfrak{g}(s) \] on \(S\) is upper semi-continuous. We are chiefly interested in the case where this function is in fact continuous, i.e. locally constant. Here are some variants of this property:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.9

Let \(S\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) be as in 2.4. Consider the following conditions:

\((\mathrm{C}_0)\) The nilpotent rank of the \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) (\(s \in S\)) is a locally constant function of \(s\).

\((\mathrm{C}_1)\) There exists locally for the fpqc topology a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

\((\mathrm{C}'_1)\) As \((\mathrm{C}_1)\), replacing "fpqc topology" by "étale topology".

\((\mathrm{C}_2)\) Condition \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is satisfied, and for every \(S'\) over \(S\), every quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'} = \mathfrak{g} \otimes_S S'\) is regular.

\((\mathrm{C}_3)\) Every \(s \in S\) has an open neighbourhood \(V\) on which the Killing polynomial of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is of the form \[ t^r(t^{n-r} + c_1 t^{n-r-1} + \cdots + c_{n-r}) \] where for every \(s \in V\), \(c_{n-r}(s) \in \mathrm{Sym}(\mathfrak{g}(s)^{\vee})\) is non-zero.

With these notations, we have the implications \[ (\mathrm{C}_3) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_2) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}'_1) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_1) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_0), \] and when \(S\) is reduced, the five conditions considered are equivalent.
Let us also note that the conditions considered are manifestly stable under base change, and of local nature for the fpqc topology.

The implications \((\mathrm{C}'_1) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_1) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_0)\) are trivial, the implication \((\mathrm{C}_0) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_3)\) when \(S\) is reduced immediate. Let us note moreover:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.10

Suppose condition \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is satisfied. Let \(\mathrm{U}\) be the set of elements of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) which are regular in their fibre. Then \(\mathrm{U}\) is open; in particular, for every section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) over \(S\), the set \(\mathrm{V}\) of the \(s \in S\) such that \(a(s) \in \mathfrak{g}(s)\) is regular, is open.

Indeed, the first assertion follows from the second (applied to \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) for every base change \(S'\) over \(S\)). For the latter, since we may assume here that \(S\) is reduced, hence \((\mathrm{C}_3)\) satisfied, it suffices to examine the Killing polynomial of \(a\) in \(\mathfrak{g}\).
The implication \((\mathrm{C}_2) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}'_1)\) now follows easily from b) in the following more precise statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.11

Suppose condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) is satisfied. Then:

a) For every \(s \in S\) and every Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}_0\) of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\), such that \(\mathfrak{d}_0\) contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) (a condition automatically satisfied if \(k(s)\) is infinite), there exist an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\), and a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}|_V\) whose fibre at \(s\) is \(\mathfrak{d}_0\). If \(S_1\) is a subprescheme of \(S\) containing \(s\), and if we have already extended \(\mathfrak{d}_0\) to a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}_1\) of \(\mathfrak{g} \otimes_S S_1\), then we can find an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) in \(S\) and a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}|_V\) such that \(\mathfrak{d} \otimes_V (S_1 \cap V)\) equals \(\mathfrak{d}_1|_{(S_1 \cap V)}\).

b) For every \(s \in S\) such that \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) contains a regular element (a condition automatically satisfied if \(k(s)\) is infinite), there exist an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}|_V\).

Statement b) follows from a) by taking \(\mathfrak{d}_0 = \mathrm{Nil}(a_0, \mathfrak{g}(s))\), \(a_0\) being a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\). To prove a), say the second formulation, one considers a regular element \(a_0\) of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) contained in \(\mathfrak{d}_0\), one extends it in a neighbourhood of \(s\) in \(S_1\) to a section of \(\mathfrak{d}_1\), and one extends the latter to a section of \(\mathfrak{g}\) in a neighbourhood of \(s\). By virtue of 2.10 this section is quasi-regular in an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\), hence regular by virtue of \((\mathrm{C}_2)\), hence by virtue of 2.6 \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a, \mathfrak{g}|_V)\) satisfies the desired conditions.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.12

I do not know whether statement 2.11 a) and b) is valid without the hypothesis of existence of regular points (when \(k(s)\) is finite).
It remains to prove the implication \((\mathrm{C}_3) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_2)\). Let us also note the following equivalent form of \((\mathrm{C}_3)\):

\((\mathrm{C}'_3)\) We have \((\mathrm{C}_0)\), i.e. the nilpotent rank of the \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) (\(s \in S\)) is locally constant, and on every open set \(V\) of \(S\) where this rank has value \(r\), the Killing polynomial of \(\mathfrak{g}|_V\) is divisible by \(t^r\).

It must be shown that this condition implies that every quasi-regular section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is regular. Taking 2.6 into account, this is contained in the following lemma (applied to the endomorphism \(\mathrm{ad}(a)\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\)), (iv) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.13

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(M\) a projective \(A\)-module of finite type, \(u\) an endomorphism of \(M\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is the direct sum of two stable modules \(M'\), \(M''\) such that \(u|_{M'}\) is nilpotent, and \(u|_{M''}\) is an automorphism of \(M''\).

  2. (ii)

    There exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that \(\mathrm{Im}\,u^n + \mathrm{Ker}\,u^n = M\).

  3. (iii)

    The nil-space \(\mathrm{Nil}(u) = \bigcup_{n>0}\mathrm{Ker}\,u^n\) is a direct factor in \(M\), and \(M = \mathrm{Nil}(u) + u(M)\).

These conditions are implied by the following one (and are equivalent to it when \(A\) is reduced):

  1. (iv)

    Locally on \(\mathrm{Spec}(A)\) (for the Zariski topology) the characteristic polynomial \(\mathrm{P}_u(t)\) of \(u\) can be put in the form \[ t^r(t^{n-r} + c_1 t^{n-r-1} + \cdots + c_{n-r}), \] where \(c_{n-r}\) is invertible.

The equivalence of (i) (ii) (iii) is immediate and included for the record. The fact that (i) implies (iv) when \(A\) is reduced comes from the fact that in this case, a nilpotent endomorphism of a projective module of rank \(r\) has characteristic polynomial \(t^r\), whereas in any case, the characteristic polynomial of an automorphism of a projective module of finite type has as constant term the determinant of \(u\) up to sign (locally on \(\mathrm{Spec}(A)\)), hence an invertible element of \(A\). Finally, to prove (iv) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), one notes that \(M\) is a module over the polynomial ring \(A[t]\), by letting \(t\) act through \(u\), and the well-known identity \[ \mathrm{P}(u) = 0 \] shows that \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathrm{P}A[t]\), hence can be considered as a module over \(A[t]/\mathrm{P}A[t]\). Now writing \(\mathrm{P} = t^r\mathrm{Q}\), where the constant term of \(\mathrm{Q}\) is invertible, one sees at once that \[ \mathrm{P}A[t] = t^r A[t] \cap \mathrm{Q}A[t], \] hence \(A[t]/\mathrm{P}A[t]\) decomposes into the product of the rings \(A[t]/t^r A[t]\) and \(A[t]/\mathrm{Q}A[t]\), whence a corresponding decomposition of \(M\) into a sum of two \(A[t]\)-modules, i.e. into a sum of two sub-\(A\)-modules \(M'\) and \(M''\) stable under \(u\); this is the decomposition considered in (i).
This completes the proof of 2.13, hence of 2.9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.14

When condition \((\mathrm{C}_3)\) of 2.9 is satisfied, the Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) are strictly nilpotent (2.2).
The proof is immediate.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.15

a) Let us point out that one can prove a converse of 2.14: \((\mathrm{C}_3)\) is equivalent to the fact that for every \(S'\) over \(S\), every quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) is regular and every Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) strictly nilpotent, or again that every quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) is contained in a strictly nilpotent Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\).

b) Contrary to the other conditions \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) to \((\mathrm{C}_2)\), condition \((\mathrm{C}_3)\) is of an "infinitesimal nature"; precisely, when \(S\) is locally noetherian, \(\mathfrak{g}\) satisfies condition \((\mathrm{C}_3)\) if and only if for every base change \(S' \to S\), with \(S'\) artinian local (if one wishes, \(S'\) the spectrum of an artinian quotient of a local ring of \(S\)), \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) satisfies the same condition. Likewise, when \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is satisfied, the condition for a section of \(\mathfrak{g}\) to be regular is of an infinitesimal nature.

c) When \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the Lie algebra of a group prescheme smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), then we will see that the conditions \((\mathrm{C}_0), (\mathrm{C}_1), (\mathrm{C}'_1), (\mathrm{C}_2)\) on \(\mathfrak{g}\) are equivalent (5.2 a)); I do not know what the situation is in general (except that, even for \(S\) artinian local, \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) does not imply \((\mathrm{C}_1)\)). However, even in the case where \(\mathfrak{g}\) comes from a \(G\), and \(S\) being artinian local, it is not true in general that \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) implies \((\mathrm{C}_3)\), for \(\mathfrak{g}\) can be nilpotent without being strictly nilpotent. One obtains an example of this fact by starting from a group scheme \(G\) smooth and affine over the spectrum \(S\) of a discrete valuation ring, such that the Lie algebra of the generic fibre is non-nilpotent (for example the generic fibre is an adjoint semi-simple group), and that of the special fibre being nilpotent (for example, the special fibre being a vector group): then for \(n\) large enough, the Lie algebra of \(G_n = G \times_S S_n\) is not strictly nilpotent, yet it is nilpotent.

Let us keep to the conditions of 2.4, and let \[ \mathscr{D} : (\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S} \longrightarrow (\mathbf{Ens}) \] be the functor defined by \[ \mathscr{D}(S') = \text{set of Cartan subalgebras of } \mathfrak{g}_{S'}. \] Let us also introduce the functor \[ X(S') = \text{set of pairs } (\mathfrak{d}, a), \text{ where } \mathfrak{d} \text{ is a Cartan subalgebra of } \mathfrak{g}_{S'} \text{ and } a \text{ a section of } \mathfrak{d}. \] We thus have two projections \((\mathfrak{d}, a) \mapsto \mathfrak{d}\) and \((\mathfrak{d}, a) \mapsto a\): \[ p : X \longrightarrow \mathscr{D} \quad \text{and} \quad \psi : X \longrightarrow \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}). \] When \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is satisfied, we also consider the open set \(\mathrm{U}\) of regular points of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) (cf. 2.10) and condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) is then expressed by the fact that the morphism \[ \psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \] induced by \(\psi\) is an isomorphism (a priori, it is a monomorphism thanks to 2.6). Note that it is trivial that the morphism \(p : X \to \mathscr{D}\) is representable by a projection of vector bundles (i.e. for every \(S\)-morphism \(S' \to \mathscr{D}\), corresponding to a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\), \(X \times_{\mathscr{D}} S'\) is representable by a vector bundle over \(S'\), namely \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{d})\)); hence if \(\mathscr{D}\) is representable, so is \(X\). Now we have:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.16

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(\mathfrak{g}\) a Lie algebra over \(S\) which is a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module of finite type; suppose condition \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) of 2.9 is satisfied.

a) The functor \(\mathscr{D}\) of Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) defined above is representable by a quasi-projective prescheme of finite presentation over \(S\). The same holds for the functor \(X\) defined above.

b) When condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) of 2.9 is satisfied, \(\mathscr{D}\) and \(X\) are smooth over \(S\), and the morphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathrm{U}\) induced by \(\psi\) is an isomorphism.

c) Still supposing condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) satisfied, let \(s \in S\), \(\mathfrak{d}_0\) a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\), corresponding to a point \(d\) of \(\mathscr{D}\) rational over \(k(s)\). Suppose that \(\mathfrak{d}_0\) contains a regular point of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) (a condition automatically satisfied if \(k(s)\) is infinite). Let \(r\) be the infinitesimal rank of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\), \(n\) its rank over \(k(s)\); then there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(d\) in \(\mathscr{D}\) which is \(S\)-isomorphic to an open subscheme \(V'\) of \(S[t_1,\ldots,t_{n-r}]\).

Proof. We may assume that \(\mathfrak{g}\) is of constant rank \(n\), and of constant nilpotent rank \(r\). The assertions made about \(X\) in a) and b) follow at once from the assertions made about \(\mathscr{D}\) and from the fact that \(X\) is a vector bundle over \(\mathscr{D}\) defined by a locally free module, and are included only for the record.

a) The functor \(\mathscr{D}\) is a subfunctor of the functor \(\mathrm{Grass}_{n-r}(\mathfrak{g})\) whose value at \(S'\) is the set of locally free quotient modules of rank \(n-r\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\), and it is well known that this last functor is representable by a prescheme projective and smooth over \(S\) (cf. for example Séminaire Cartan 1960/61, Exp. 12, Nos 2 and 3 (whose constructions carry over as they stand to the case of preschemes))23. We are thus reduced to a relative problem, namely the following: given a locally free quotient module of rank \(n-r\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), or what amounts to the same, a submodule \(\mathfrak{d}\) of rank \(r\) which is locally a direct factor, to represent the following functor: \(F(S') = \varnothing\) if \(\mathfrak{d}_{S'}\) is not a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\), \(F(S') = \{\varnothing\}\) otherwise. In fact, we are going to see that \(F\) is representable by a subprescheme of finite presentation of \(S\) (which will show that \(\mathscr{D} \to \mathrm{Grass}\) is representable by an immersion of finite presentation, and will finish proving a)). One begins by expressing the condition that \(\mathfrak{d}_{S'}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\); one sees right away that this is expressed by the fact that \(S' \to S\) factors through a certain closed subprescheme \(S_1\) of \(S\), of finite presentation over \(S\) (whose local equations over \(S\) can be written down immediately using a basis of \(\mathfrak{g}\) adapted to the submodule \(\mathfrak{d}\)). We may therefore assume that we already have \(S = S_1\). One must next express that \(\mathfrak{d}_{S'}\) contains, locally for fpqc, a quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\), and for this one considers \(V = \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{d}) \cap \mathrm{U}\), where \(\mathrm{U}\) is the open set of regular points of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) (2.10); then the structural morphism \(V \to S\) being smooth and quasi-compact, its image \(S_1\) is an open subset of \(S\) and the immersion morphism \(S_1 \to S\) is quasi-compact, i.e. of finite presentation. The condition considered on \(S'\) is then expressed by saying that \(S' \to S\) factors through \(S_1\). We are thus reduced to the case \(S = S_1\), and, using descent theory, to the case where \(\mathfrak{d}\) admits a section \(a\) which is a quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Finally one must express that the section \(a_{S'}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) deduced from \(a\) satisfies: \(\mathrm{ad}(a_{S'})_{\mathfrak{g}_{S'}/\mathfrak{d}_{S'}}\) bijective, which again amounts to saying that \(S' \to S\) factors through a certain open subprescheme of finite presentation of \(S\), namely \(S_{\mathrm{D}}\), where \(\mathrm{D}\) is the determinant of \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\). But then one sees right away that \(\mathfrak{d}|_{S_{\mathrm{D}}}\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}|_{S_{\mathrm{D}}}\), hence \(S_{\mathrm{D}}\) represents the functor \(F\), which proves a).
b) Is immediate thanks to 2.11 a) and XI 1.5. Of course b) is also a consequence of the more precise statement c).

c) Let \(a_0\) be a regular point of \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\), contained in \(\mathfrak{d}_0\); let us extend it to a section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), over an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\); we may evidently assume \(V = S\). On the other hand let \(M_0\) be a complement of the vector space \(\mathfrak{d}_0\) in \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\); then in an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(S\) there exists a submodule \(M\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), a direct factor of \(\mathfrak{g}|_V\), such that \(M(s) = M_0\), and we may again assume \(V = S\). Now let \(V\) be the subfunctor of \(\mathscr{D}\) such that \(V(S')\) is the set of Cartan subalgebras \(\mathfrak{d}'\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) which satisfy the two following conditions:
1°) \(\mathfrak{d}'\) complementary to \(M_{S'}\), and
2°) the unique section of \((a_{S'} + M_{S'}) \cap \mathfrak{d}'\) is a regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\).

Condition 1°) corresponds to an open subscheme \(V_1\) of \(\mathscr{D}\) (induced by the open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Grass}_{n-r}(\mathfrak{g})\) defined by the same condition 1°)); the conjunction of 1°) and 2°) corresponds to an open subscheme of \(V_1\) by virtue of 2.10 and \((\mathrm{C}_2)\). Hence \(V\) is represented by an open subprescheme \(V\) of \(\mathscr{D}\), evidently containing \(d\).
On the other hand let \(V'\) be the subfunctor of \(\mathrm{W}(M)\) defined by \[ V'(S') = \text{set of sections } u' \text{ of } M_{S'} \text{ such that:} \]
(i) \(a_{S'} + u'\) is a regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\), and
(ii) the unique Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}'\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) which contains \(a_{S'} + u'\) is a complement of \(M_{S'}\).

Then condition (i) corresponds to an open subprescheme \(V'_1\) of \(\mathrm{W}(M)\), namely the inverse image of the open set \(\mathrm{U}\) of regular points of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) (cf. 2.10) under the translation morphism \(m \mapsto a + m\). The conjunction of (i) and (ii) corresponds to an open subscheme \(V'\) of \(V'_1\), namely the inverse image of \(V\) under the evident morphism \(V'_1 \to \mathscr{D}\) (associating with \(u'\) the unique Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}'\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_{S'}\) which contains \(a_{S'} + u'\)). The restriction of this last morphism to \(V'\) is a morphism \[ V' \longrightarrow V, \] which is evidently an isomorphism. This proves c).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.17

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be a finite-dimensional Lie algebra over a field \(k\). Then the scheme \(\mathscr{D}\) of Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (2.16 a)) is quasi-projective, smooth and irreducible. When \(\mathfrak{g}\) contains a regular element (for example when \(k\) is infinite) \(\mathscr{D}\) is a rational variety, i.e. its function field is a purely transcendental extension of \(k\).

The fact that \(\mathscr{D}\) is irreducible comes from the fact that we have a surjective morphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathscr{D}\), and \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\) is irreducible, being isomorphic to the open set \(\mathrm{U}\) of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\). The assertion about the function field is an immediate consequence of c).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.18

I do not know whether this conclusion remains true if \(k\) is finite, without supposing that \(\mathfrak{g}\) contains a regular point, compare 2.12. One can prove that this is so when \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the Lie algebra of an algebraic group \(G\) smooth over \(k\), at least when \(G/\text{radical}\) is an "adjoint" semi-simple group, by using a result of Chevalley mentioned below (cf. Appendix). It is plausible that this result remains valid without restriction on \(G\); for this it would suffice that the cited result of Chevalley be proved for every semi-simple algebraic group (not necessarily adjoint).

footnotes.html: cf. also EGA I, 2<sup>nd</sup> edition (to appear with North Holland Publishing Cie).
N.D.E.: See §I.1.3 of M. Demazure and P. Gabriel, Groupes algébriques, Masson (1970).

pages.title: 3. Subgroups of type (C) of group preschemes over an arbitrary prescheme

blocks.html: 3. Subgroups of type (C) of group preschemes over an arbitrary prescheme

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(G\) a smooth \(S\)-group prescheme, \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra (which is a locally free module of finite type over \(S\)), \(\mathfrak{h}\) a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) which is (as a module) locally a direct factor in \(\mathfrak{g}\), and such that for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{h}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\). Let \(a\) be a quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (2.5). Then \[ M_a = \mathrm{Transp}_G(a,\mathfrak{h}) \] (the subfunctor of \(G\) whose points with values in \(S'\) are the \(g \in G(S')\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\cdot a_{S'} \in \Gamma(S', \mathfrak{h}_{S'})\))4 is representable by a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\), whose structural morphism to \(S\) is surjective.

Consider the canonical morphism \[ \varphi : G \underset{S}{\times} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}) \] given by \((g,x) \mapsto \mathrm{ad}(g)\cdot x\); then \(M_a\) is \(S\)-isomorphic to \(\varphi^{-1}(a)\), the inverse image of \(a\) (considered as a section of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) over \(S\)) under \(\varphi\). For the smoothness of \(M_a\) it will therefore suffice to show that \(\varphi\) is smooth at the points of \(G \times_S \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) lying over \(\mathrm{Im}(a)\); more generally \(\varphi\) is smooth at every point lying over a point of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) which is regular in its fibre \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}(s))\) over \(S\). To see this, since the source and target of \(\varphi\) are smooth, hence flat and locally of finite presentation over \(S\), we are reduced to making the verification fibre by fibre, which brings us to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, \(G\) thus being a locally algebraic group over \(k\), \(\mathfrak{h}\) a subalgebra of its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\), containing a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and \(a\) a regular point of \(\mathfrak{g}\). We may evidently assume (taking into account that \(\varphi\) is a \(G\)-morphism) that the point of \(G \times \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) under consideration is of the form \((e,a)\). We may evidently assume \(G\) connected, hence of finite type over \(k\), but then our assertion is none other than XIII 5.4. Moreover, the fact that \(M_a\) is a closed subprescheme of \(G\) (of finite presentation over \(S\)) is trivial, since \(M_a\) is the inverse image of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) under the morphism \(g \mapsto \mathrm{ad}(g)\cdot a\) from \(G\) to \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\). The surjectivity of the structural morphism \(M_a \to S\) reduces likewise to the case of an algebraically closed base field, but then \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) by hypothesis, which is therefore conjugate to the Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) by the conjugation theorem XIII 6.1 a), hence \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a conjugate of \(a\). This completes the proof of 3.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Let \(G\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) be as in 3.1, with \(G\) of finite type over \(S\); let \(\mathfrak{k}\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) be two Lie subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\), locally direct factors (as modules), and suppose that one of the two following hypotheses holds:

a) For every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{k}_{\overline{s}}\) is nilpotent and contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\); the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{h}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\).

b) \(\mathfrak{k}\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(\mathfrak{k},\mathfrak{h})\) is a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\); moreover, in case a), its structural morphism to \(S\) is surjective.

The fact that the transporter is representable by a closed subprescheme of finite presentation of \(G\) is immediate, and left to the reader. Let us first prove smoothness in case a). Suppose first that there exists a section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{k}\) which is quasi-regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then it suffices to apply 3.1 and the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

Under the conditions of 3.2 a), if \(a\) is a section of \(\mathfrak{k}\) quasi-regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\), then we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(\mathfrak{k},\mathfrak{h}) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(a,\mathfrak{h}). \]

Indeed, taking the definitions into account, this amounts to showing that if \(a\) is moreover a section of \(\mathfrak{h}\), then \(\mathfrak{k} \subset \mathfrak{h}\). Now since by hypothesis \(\mathfrak{k}\) is locally nilpotent, it follows from 2.1 that \(\mathfrak{k} \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\); on the other hand \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) \subset \mathfrak{h}\) because \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective (being so fibre by fibre by virtue of XIII 4.8 b)). Whence the conclusion. In the general case, one reduces to the case where \(S\) is affine noetherian by the usual standard procedure, then to the case where \(S\) is local artinian (smoothness being a property of infinitesimal nature), and by flat descent to the case where its residue field \(k_0\) is infinite, hence where the fibre \(\mathscr{K}_0\) admits an element which is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}_0\). One lifts this element to an element of \(\mathscr{K} = \Gamma(\mathfrak{k})\), which brings us back to the preceding case. Thus, in case a) we have proved the smoothness of the transporter; as for the fact that its structural morphism is surjective, this reduces to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, hence where \(\mathscr{K}\) contains a regular point of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and one applies 3.3 and 3.1.

To prove b), one is reduced, by definition of smoothness (XI 1.1), to proving that if \(S\) is affine, \(S_0\) a subscheme defined by a nilpotent quasi-coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), \(g_0\) an element of \(G(S_0)\) which transports \(\mathfrak{k}_0\) into \(\mathfrak{h}_0\), then \(g_0\) lifts to an element \(g\) of \(G(S)\) which transports \(\mathfrak{k}\) into \(\mathfrak{h}\). But the hypothesis made on \(g_0\) implies that we are under the conditions of a), already treated. This completes the proof.

Of course, when in 3.2 b) \(\mathfrak{h}\) satisfies the stronger hypothesis of 3.1, then (and only then) the structural morphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(\mathfrak{d},\mathfrak{h}) \to S\) is surjective. Using the Hensel lemma XI 1.10, one concludes from 3.1 and 3.2:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Under the conditions of 3.1 for \(G\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) and supposing \(G\) of finite type over \(S\):

a) for every quasi-regular section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), there exists locally for the étale topology a conjugate of \(a\) which is a section of \(\mathfrak{h}\).

b) For every Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), \(\mathfrak{d}\) is locally for the étale topology conjugate to a subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\).
In particular, when \(\mathfrak{h}\) is itself a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let \(G\) be a smooth \(S\)-group prescheme of finite type, \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra, \(\mathfrak{d}\) and \(\mathfrak{d}'\) two Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(\mathfrak{d},\mathfrak{d}')\) is identical to the strict transporter of \(\mathfrak{d}\) into \(\mathfrak{d}'\), and is a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\), with surjective structural morphism. Locally for the étale topology, \(\mathfrak{d}\) and \(\mathfrak{d}'\) are conjugate.

The fact that the transporter is here identical to the strict transporter comes trivially from the fact that \(\mathfrak{d}\) and \(\mathfrak{d}'\) are locally direct factors in \(\mathfrak{g}\), and have the same rank at each point. Hence 3.5 is a special case of 3.4. In particular, if \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathfrak{d}'\):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

Let \(G\) be as in 3.5, and let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then \(\mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d})\) is a closed group subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\), whose Lie algebra is identical to \(\mathfrak{d}\).
Indeed, this last assertion amounts to saying that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is its own normalizer in \(\mathfrak{g}\), which follows at once from the fact that it is true fibre by fibre.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(G\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) be as in 3.5. Then the conditions \((\mathrm{C}_2)\), \((\mathrm{C}'_1)\), \((\mathrm{C}_1)\) of 2.9 are equivalent; in other words, if \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits locally for the fpqc topology a Cartan subalgebra, then every quasi-regular section of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is regular.

Indeed, let \(a\) be a quasi-regular section; let us prove that it is regular. The question being local for the fpqc topology, we may assume that \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\). By virtue of 3.4 a), \(a\) is then locally for the étale topology conjugate to a section of \(\mathfrak{d}\), which brings us to the case where \(a\) is a section of \(\mathfrak{d}\), where the conclusion is trivial from the definition.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.8

Let \(G\) be a group prescheme smooth over a prescheme \(S\). We call a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\) a group subprescheme \(D\) of \(G\), smooth over \(S\), with connected fibres, such that \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Lie}(D)\) is a Cartan subalgebra (2.4) of \(\mathfrak{g} = \mathrm{Lie}(G)\), i.e. such that for every \(s \in S\), \(D_s\) is a subgroup of type (C) of the algebraic group \(G_s\) (XIII 6.2).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.9

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth and of finite presentation, \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra. Then:

a) The map \[ D \mapsto \mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Lie}(D) \] establishes a one-to-one correspondence between subgroups of type (C) of \(G\), and Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

b) If \(D\) and \(\mathfrak{d}\) correspond to each other, we have \[ \mathrm{Norm}_G(D) = \mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d}), \] which is a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\), and we have \[ D = \mathrm{Norm}_G(D)^0 = \mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d})^0. \]

c) Two subgroups of type (C) \(D\) and \(D'\) of \(G\) are conjugate locally for the étale topology.

Proof. Let \(D\) be a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\), and \(\mathfrak{d}\) its Lie algebra; then \(D \subset \mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d})\), and by virtue of definition 3.8 and 3.6 this is an inclusion of group preschemes smooth over \(S\), inducing an isomorphism on the Lie algebras. Since \(D\) has connected fibres, we therefore have \(D = \mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d})^0\). Hence the map considered in a) is injective. So let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\); then by virtue of 3.6 \(\mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d}) = N\) is a closed group subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\), admitting \(\mathfrak{d}\) as Lie algebra. Since \(G\) is of finite presentation over \(S\), so is \(N\), hence (as was pointed out in XII after 7.3) the union of the connected components of the fibres of \(N\) is the underlying set of an open group subprescheme \(N^0\) of \(N\), which is evidently a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\) having Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{d}\). This proves a); the first assertion of b) follows from it at once, and the formula \(D = \mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{d})^0\) has already been proved. Finally, c) follows from a) and 3.5.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.10

Suppose that \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits locally for the fpqc topology a Cartan subalgebra (or what amounts to the same by virtue of 3.9 a), that \(G\) admits locally for the fpqc topology a subgroup of type (C)). Consider the functor \[ \mathscr{D} : (\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S} \longrightarrow (\mathbf{Ens}) \] defined by \(\mathscr{D}(S') = \) set of subgroups of type (C) of \(G_{S'}\). Then this functor is representable by a prescheme quasi-projective and smooth over \(S\), with connected geometric fibres. When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), \(G\) an algebraic group smooth over \(k\), and \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits a regular point (a condition automatically satisfied if \(k\) is infinite), \(\mathscr{D}\) is a rational variety over \(k\).

Indeed, by virtue of 3.9 a), the functor \(\mathscr{D}\) is canonically isomorphic to the functor considered in 2.16; on the other hand by virtue of 3.7 condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) is satisfied. Hence 3.10 follows from 2.16 and 2.17.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.11

Let \(D\) be a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\), and let \(N\) be its normalizer in \(G\). Then the quotient sheaf \(G/N\) is canonically isomorphic to the functor \(\mathscr{D}\) of 3.10, hence representable by a prescheme quasi-projective and smooth over \(S\), with connected geometric fibres.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.12

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme, smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), \(H\), \(K\) two group subpreschemes, smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), \(K\) having connected fibres, \(\mathfrak{g}\), \(\mathfrak{k}\), \(\mathfrak{h}\) the corresponding Lie algebras. We suppose that one of the two following conditions is realized for the geometric fibres of the latter:

a) For every \(s \in S\), \(\mathfrak{k}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\), and \(\mathfrak{h}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\).

b) For every \(s \in S\), \(\mathfrak{k}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\). Under these conditions, in order to have \(H \supset K\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{k}\).

Of course, we only have to prove that if \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{k}\), then \(H \supset K\). In case b), the inclusion \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{k}\) shows that we are in fact under the conditions of a), so it suffices to prove a). Proceeding as in 3.2 a) by reduction to the case \(S\) artinian local, we are reduced to the case where there exists a section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{k}\) which is quasi-regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\). In this case, proceeding as in XIII 5.5, we are reduced to the following statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.13

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), \(H\) a group subprescheme of \(G\) smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) the Lie algebras, \(a\) a section of \(\mathfrak{h}\) which is quasi-regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\). We suppose that for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{h}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\). Let \[ M_a = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(a,\mathfrak{h}), \] which is a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\) (cf. 3.1), so that \(M_a^0\) (the union of the connected components of the identity element in the fibres of \(M_a\)) is an open subset of \(M_a\), which we endow with the structure induced by \(M_a\). We then have \(H^0 = M_a^0\).

Evidently, we have \(H \subset M_a\) whence \(H^0 \subset M_a^0\). Since this is an inclusion of preschemes smooth over \(S\), to prove that it is an equality we are reduced to checking it on the fibres, which brings us to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, a case which was seen in XIII 5.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.14

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation, \(D\) a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\), \(H\) a group subprescheme of \(G\) smooth and of finite presentation over \(S\), \(\mathfrak{d}\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) their Lie algebras. Then we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(D,H) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(\mathfrak{d},\mathfrak{h}), \] and this functor is representable by a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\).
Indeed, the identity between the two transporters follows from 3.12 b), which makes it possible to apply 3.2.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.15

Let \(G\), \(H\) be as in 3.14 and suppose that for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{h}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\). Suppose moreover that \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits locally for the fpqc topology a Cartan subalgebra. Then \(H\) contains a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\).

By virtue of 3.7 and 3.9 a), \(G\) admits locally for the étale topology a subgroup of type (C), so we may assume that \(G\) admits such a subgroup, say \(D\). Then the hypothesis on \(\mathfrak{h}\) also means that the structural morphism of the transporter considered in 3.14 is surjective (taking into account the conjugation theorem XIII 6.1 a)). One then concludes by the Hensel lemma XI 1.10.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.16

Let \(G\), \(H\), \(K\) be as in 3.12 a); suppose moreover that for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{k}_{\overline{s}}\) is nilpotent (i.e. \(\mathfrak{k}\) is locally nilpotent). Then we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(K,H) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(\mathfrak{k},\mathfrak{h}), \] and this functor is representable by a closed subprescheme of \(G\) smooth over \(S\), with structural morphism to \(S\) surjective. \(H\) contains locally for the étale topology a subgroup conjugate to \(K\).

The identity of the two transporters is again contained in 3.12 a), the assertion about its structure is then none other than 3.2 a), and the last assertion of 3.16 is then a consequence of the Hensel lemma.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.17

a) In 3.12 and 3.16, one can replace the hypothesis that \(K\) is smooth over \(S\) by the following weaker hypothesis: the sheaf \(e_K^*(\Omega^1_{K/S}) = \omega^1_K\) of relative 1-differentials along the unit section is locally free. In this way, 3.12 contains XIII 5.5.

b) Let \(G\), \(\mathfrak{g}\), \(\mathfrak{h}\) be as in 3.1, with \(G\) of finite presentation over \(S\). Then \(N = \mathrm{Norm}_G(\mathfrak{h})\) is not necessarily smooth over \(S\) along the unit section, or what amounts to the same, there does not necessarily exist a group subprescheme \(H\) of \(G\) smooth over \(S\) whose Lie algebra is \(\mathfrak{h}\), even if \(S\) is the spectrum of a field. When such an \(H\) exists, so that we then have (taking \(H\) with connected fibres) \(N = \mathrm{Norm}_G(H)\), I do not know whether \(N\) is smooth over \(S\). In this question, one can evidently reduce to the case where \(S\) is local artinian.
To finish this section, let us examine the case where \(G\) is "semi-simple" over \(S\):

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.18

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(G\) an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth over \(S\), whose geometric fibres are "adjoint" semi-simple groups, i.e. semi-simple with reductive center (XII 4.1 and 4.4) reduced to the unit group. Then the subgroups of type (C) of \(G\) are identical to its maximal tori, hence also to its Cartan subgroups (XII 3.1).

Taking the definitions into account, one reduces to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, and to proving then that for a maximal torus \(T\) of \(G\), the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{t}\) of \(T\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), that is to say (taking into account the inequality nilpotent rank of \(\mathfrak{g} \geqslant\) nilpotent rank of \(G = \dim T = \mathrm{rank}\,\mathfrak{t} = r\)) that there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{t}\) with \(\dim \mathrm{Nil}(x,\mathfrak{g}) = r\). Since \(\mathfrak{t}\) is abelian and a fortiori nilpotent, it amounts to the same to say that there exists an \(a \in \mathfrak{t}\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{t}}\) is injective (XIII 5.7 a)). Now consider the characters \(\alpha\) of \(T\) which occur in the representation induced on \(\mathfrak{g}\) by the adjoint representation of \(G\). The structure theory of the semi-simple group \(G\) (BIBLE, 13 th. 1 a) and th. 3, cor. 2), more precisely of the "big cell", the semi-direct product of \(T\) and of subgroups \(P_{\alpha}\) isomorphic to the additive group \(\mathbb{G}_a\), invariant under \(T\) and corresponding to the "root" characters of \(G\) for the torus \(T\), shows that the eigenspace of \(\mathfrak{g}\) relative to the unit character is none other than \(\mathfrak{t}\), and the other eigenspaces are of dimension 1, the associated characters \(\alpha\) being none other than the roots of \(G\) for \(T\). By virtue of the computation of the reductive center of \(G\) as the intersection of the kernels of the characters of \(T\) which occur in the adjoint representation of \(G\) (XII 4.8), one sees that the fact that \(G\) is adjoint is interpreted by the fact that the roots generate the lattice \(M = \mathrm{Hom}(T,\mathbb{G}_m)\). Now a well-known lemma of root theory tells us that every root is part of a system of simple roots, hence of a basis of the group generated by the roots, and consequently of a basis of the dual \(M\) of \(T\)5. One concludes:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.19

If \(G\) is an adjoint semi-simple algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(T\) a maximal torus of \(G\), then for every root \(\alpha\) of \(G\) with respect to \(T\), \(\alpha : T \to \mathbb{G}_m\), the corresponding homomorphism \(\alpha' : \mathfrak{t} \to k\) is non-zero.

This result is essentially equivalent to theorem 3.18, for, for a \(t \in \mathfrak{t}\), \(\mathrm{ad}(t)\) is semi-simple and its eigenvalues in \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{t}\) are none other than the \(\alpha'(t)\), hence \(\mathrm{ad}(t)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{t}}\) is injective if and only if the \(\alpha'(t)\) are \(\neq 0\), and there exists a \(t \in \mathfrak{t}\) having this property if and only if all the \(\alpha'\) are \(\neq 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.20

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(G\) an \(S\)-group prescheme, smooth, of finite presentation over \(S\), whose geometric fibres are connected and reductive algebraic groups (i.e. extensions of a semi-simple group by a torus). Then for every \(s \in S\) there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(G|_U\) admits a maximal torus.6

Indeed, we will see in XVI that the hypothesis just made on \(G\) implies that \(G\) is affine over \(S\) and that the reductive rank is locally constant; hence (XII 4.7 c)) \(G\) admits a reductive center \(Z\), \(G' = G/Z\) is a group smooth and affine over \(S\) whose reductive center is reduced to the unit group, and finally the maximal tori of \(G\) and of \(G'\) are in one-to-one correspondence. Moreover, one sees at once that the geometric fibres of \(G'\) are connected semi-simple groups, and moreover adjoint by definition (their reductive center being trivial). Hence it suffices to restrict to the case where \(G\) is semi-simple and adjoint, and by virtue of 3.18 we are reduced to finding an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) and a subgroup of type (C) of \(G|_U\), or what amounts to the same (3.9 a)) a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}|_U\). In fact, this is possible if \(k(s)\) is infinite, for by virtue of 3.7 \(\mathfrak{g}\) satisfies condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) of 2.9, so one can apply 2.11 b). In fact, the statement 3.20 remains valid without supposing \(k(s)\) infinite. For this, it suffices to know that for every adjoint semi-simple group \(G\) over a finite field \(k\), the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) of \(G\) contains a regular element. Now this statement was proved by Chevalley (using the properties of the Coxeter element of the Weyl group…), cf. the Appendix below by J.-P. Serre.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.21

a) The statement 3.20 remains valid, with essentially the same proof, upon replacing \(G\) in it by a group subprescheme \(H\) closed, smooth over \(S\), having everywhere the same rank as \(G\) (for example a "parabolic subgroup" of \(G\)), provided that \(k(s)\) is infinite. I do not know whether here again the hypothesis that \(k(s)\) be infinite is superfluous. One can show that this is so at least if \(H\) is parabolic, thanks to the construction of the radical \(U\) of \(H\) and of the quotient \(H/U\), which brings us back to the semi-simple case. Unfortunately, the method of regular elements seems powerless here, for one easily constructs examples (for instance with the projective group and its "standard" Borel subgroup) where the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) of \(H\) contains no regular element.

b) The proof of 3.20 in fact shows a more precise result (by invoking 3.9 b)) in the case where \(k(s)\) is infinite, namely that every maximal torus \(T_0\) of \(G_s\) comes from a maximal torus \(T\) over an open neighbourhood of \(s\). I do not know whether the statement remains valid when \(k(s)\) is no longer supposed infinite, the difficulty evidently coming from the fact that the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{t}_0\) of \(T_0\) does not in general contain a regular element of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}_0\) of the fibre \(G_s\). An affirmative answer to this problem would imply the following statement (which is proved only in the case of an infinite residue field or when \(A\) is separated and complete): Let \(A\) be a local ring, with residue field \(k\), \(M\) an "Azumaya algebra" over \(A\), i.e. an algebra such that \(M\) is a free module of finite type over \(A\), and \(M_0 = M \otimes_A k\) a central simple algebra over \(k\), \(D_0\) a commutative subalgebra of \(M_0\) separable over \(k\), such that \([M_0 : k] = ([D_0 : k])^2\); then there exists a commutative subalgebra \(D\) of \(M\), which is a module direct factor of \(M\) and such that \(D \otimes_A k = D_0\) (?). (Note that giving \(M\) is equivalent to giving a principal homogeneous bundle under the projective group \(\mathrm{PGL}(n)_A\), whence an "inner" twisted form \(G\) of \(\mathrm{PGL}(n)\), and the maximal tori of \(G\) correspond one-to-one to the commutative subalgebras \(D\) of \(M\), étale over \(A\), of rank \(n\) over \(A\).)

c) Applying 3.20 to the centralizer of a subtorus \(Q\) of \(G\) (\(G\) a reductive \(S\)-group prescheme), one deduces that every such \(Q\) is contained, locally for the Zariski topology, in a maximal torus of \(G\).

footnotes.html: This result, as well as 2.11 on which it relies, generalizes immediately to the case where \(s\) is replaced by a finite subset of \(S\), contained in an affine open set.

blocks.html: 4. A digression on Borel subgroups

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\). We call a Borel subgroup of \(G\) a smooth connected solvable algebraic subgroup which is maximal for these properties.

When \(G\) is affine, we thus recover the terminology of BIBLE 6 def. 1. Let us note right away that if \(Z\) is a connected and smooth subgroup of \(G\), contained in the center (or more generally, solvable and normal), then for every Borel subgroup \(B\) of \(G\), the image \(BZ\) of \(B \times Z\) under the morphism \((b,z) \mapsto bz\) from \(B \times Z\) to \(G\) is a smooth solvable connected subgroup of \(G\) containing \(B\), hence identical to \(B\), hence \(B\) contains \(Z\), hence \(B\) is the inverse image of an algebraic subgroup \(B'\) of \(G' = G/Z\), and it is immediate that \(B'\) is a Borel subgroup of \(G'\). Taking \(Z = \mathrm{Centr}(G^0)^0_{\mathrm{red}}\), \(G' = G/Z\) is affine (XII 6.1) and it follows that the Borel subgroups of \(G'\) are conjugate, and for such a \(B'\), \(G'/B'\) is a projective variety (BIBLE 6 th. 4). Consequently:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let \(G\) be as in 4.1. Then the Borel subgroups of \(G\) are conjugate. If \(B\) is a Borel subgroup, then \(G/B\) is a projective variety. The maximal tori of \(B\) (resp. the Cartan subgroups of \(B\), \(G\) being connected) are maximal tori of \(G\) (resp. Cartan subgroups of \(G\)).

It remains to prove the last assertion, and we may evidently assume \(G = G^0\). For the Cartan subgroups, it follows from the analogous assertion in \(G'\) (BIBLE 6 th. 4 cor. 4) and from XII 6.6 e). For the maximal tori, it is deduced from the preceding one, since by XII 6.6 c) the maximal tori of a smooth algebraic group are the maximal tori of its Cartan subgroups.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Suppose \(G\) is connected. Then every element of \(G\) is contained in a Borel subgroup of \(G\).
One is reduced to the same statement in \(G'\), which is well known (BIBLE 6 th. 5 d)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Let \(B\) be a Borel subgroup of \(G\), \(C\) a Cartan subgroup of \(B\), \(N\) its normalizer in \(G\); then \(N \cap B = C\).

Indeed, \(N \cap B = \mathrm{Norm}_B(C)\), so we are reduced to showing that when \(G\) is connected and solvable, then a Cartan subgroup \(C\) of \(G\) is its own connected normalizer. Now with the preceding notations, \(C\) is the inverse image of a Cartan subgroup \(C'\) of \(G'\), so we are reduced to the case where \(G\) is affine. Since we know that the normalizer of a Cartan subgroup is smooth (\(C\) being its own connected normalizer, cf. for example XII 6.6 c)), it suffices to see that \(C\) and \(N\) have the same points with values in \(k\), which is none other than BIBLE th. 6 d).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.5

Let \(G\) be a group prescheme smooth of finite presentation over a prescheme \(S\). We call a Borel subgroup of \(G\) any group subprescheme \(B\) of finite presentation of \(G\), smooth over \(S\), such that for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(B_{\overline{s}}\) is a Borel subgroup of \(G_{\overline{s}}\).

This is therefore, as one verifies at once, a notion stable under base change, and of local nature for the fpqc topology (for if \(k'\) is an algebraically closed extension of an algebraically closed field \(k\), then an algebraic subgroup \(B\) of the algebraic group \(G\) smooth over \(k\) is a Borel subgroup of \(G\) if and only if \(B_{k'}\) is one of \(G_{k'}\)). It follows from this definition that if \(G\) is a smooth algebraic group over an arbitrary field \(k\), \(B\) a Borel subgroup of \(G\), then \(G/B\) is a projective variety, every maximal torus \(T\) of \(B\) is a maximal torus of \(G\), its normalizer in \(B\) is identical to its centralizer, and is a Cartan subgroup of \(G\) when \(G\) is connected.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 4.6

Unfortunately, it is no longer true in general (even if \(G\) is affine over \(S\) and \(S\) is the spectrum of the algebra of dual numbers over an algebraically closed field \(k\)) that two Borel subgroups of \(G\) are conjugate locally for the fpqc topology. As a consequence of this regrettable fact, let us point out that if \(G\) is a smooth, affine, connected algebraic group over a non-perfect field \(k\), it is in general not possible to define in a natural way a homogeneous space \(D\) under \(G\) playing the role of a flag variety, i.e. of the variety of Borel subgroups of \(G\) (which, over the algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\), would thus be isomorphic to \(G_{\overline{k}}/\overline{B}\), where \(\overline{B}\) is a Borel subgroup of \(G_{\overline{k}}\)). Indeed, when the quotient of \(G_{\overline{k}}\) by its radical \(\overline{R}\) is an adjoint semi-simple group, then the kernel of \(G_{\overline{k}} \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k(\overline{D})\) is the radical of \(G_{\overline{k}}\), so if \(\overline{D}\) comes from a homogeneous space \(D\) under \(G\), the radical \(\overline{R}\) comes from a subgroup \(R\) of \(G\). Now one easily constructs examples where \(G_{\overline{k}}/\overline{R}\) is adjoint but \(\overline{R}\) is not "defined over \(k\)". It is easy to see that under these conditions, the functor \(\mathscr{B} : (\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S} \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) such that \(\mathscr{B}(S') = \) set of Borel subgroups of \(G_{S'}\), is not representable by a smooth \(S\)-prescheme. From the infinitesimal point of view (III §3), the failure of the conjugation theorem is expressed by the fact that if \(B\) is a Borel subgroup of the smooth algebraic group \(G\), the cohomology group \(\mathrm{H}^1(B, \mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{b})\)7 can be different from zero.

We will see on the other hand in a later exposé that when \(G\) is semi-simple, or more generally reductive, such unpleasant phenomena do not occur. It is no doubt these phenomena, as well as the absence of good existence theorems, which explain why Borel subgroups play only a relatively unobtrusive role in the study of general group schemes from the scheme-theoretic point of view, whereas they will dominate the theory of semi-simple group schemes in the later exposés.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8 8

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation with connected fibres, \(B\) a Borel subgroup of \(G\); then \(B\) is identical to its own normalizer, and it is a closed subprescheme of \(G\).

By virtue of XII 7.10, we are reduced to proving that over an algebraically closed field \(k\), every element of \(G(k)\) which normalizes \(B\) is in \(B(k)\), which for \(G\) affine is a fundamental result of Chevalley (BIBLE 9 th. 1); the general case reduces to it at once by the reduction already used in 4.2.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 4.8.1

One can generalize definition 4.5, by also introducing the notion of a parabolic subgroup of \(G\): one calls thus a group subprescheme \(P\) of \(G\), smooth and of finite presentation over \(S\), such that for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(P_{\overline{s}}\) is a parabolic subgroup of \(G_{\overline{s}}\), i.e. contains a Borel subgroup of \(G_{\overline{s}}\). Proposition 4.8 extends (with the same proof by reduction to the "set-theoretic" statement, which is known) to the case of a parabolic subgroup \(P\) of \(G\). Let us note the following consequence of this result (cf. XVI): If \(P\) is a parabolic subgroup of \(G\), then \(G/P\) is representable by a quasi-projective prescheme of finite presentation over \(S\) (N.B. \(G\) is assumed to have connected fibres). Moreover \(G/P\) is evidently smooth over \(S\), and furthermore has connected and proper geometric fibres, from which one can easily conclude, using EGA III 5.5.1, that \(D = G/P\) is in fact proper, hence projective, over \(S\). Moreover, if its relative dimension is \(n\), it is known that the invertible sheaf \(\Omega^n_{D/S}\) is such that its inverse induces ample sheaves on the geometric fibres of \(D/S\), hence (EGA III 4.7.1) \((\Omega^n_{D/S})^{-1}\) is ample for \(D\) relative to \(S\).

One sees easily, by reduction to the affine case and to the case of an algebraically closed base field, that if \(u : G \to G'\) is an epimorphism of smooth algebraic groups, then for every Borel subgroup \(B\) of \(G\), \(u(B) = B'\) is a Borel subgroup of \(G'\). We are interested in the case where one obtains in this way a bijective correspondence between Borel subgroups of \(G\) and of \(G'\):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.9

Let \(G\), \(G'\) be two \(S\)-group preschemes smooth of finite presentation with connected fibres, \(u : G \to G'\) a faithfully flat, i.e. surjective, homomorphism of groups9. We suppose that we are in one of the two following cases (where we have set \(N = \mathrm{Ker}\,u\)):

a) \(N\) is central in \(G\).

b) \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and if \(\overline{k}\) denotes an algebraic closure of it, \(N_{\overline{k}} = \mathrm{Ker}\,u_{\overline{k}}\) is contained in the radical of \(G_{\overline{k}}\), i.e. in the largest smooth connected solvable normal subgroup of \(G_{\overline{k}}\).

Then the map \(B' \mapsto u^{-1}(B')\) induces a bijection of the set of Borel subgroups of \(G'\) with the analogous set for \(G\).

Case b) follows at once from the correspondence between algebraic subgroups of \(G'\) and algebraic subgroups of \(G\) containing \(N\), and the fact that when \(k\) is algebraically closed, the Borel subgroups of \(G\) contain the radical of \(G\) (which is immediate by the reasoning preceding 4.2).

Let us prove case a). By virtue of XII 7.12, the map \(H' \mapsto H = u^{-1}(H')\) establishes a one-to-one correspondence between group subpreschemes \(H'\) of \(G'\) which are smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), with connected fibres, and which have at every \(s \in S\) the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank as \(G'\), and the group subpreschemes \(H\) of \(G\) having the analogous properties. Now the Borel subgroups (of \(G'\), or of \(G\)) have the properties in question. It remains to prove that if \(H'\), \(H\) correspond to each other, then \(H'\) is a Borel subgroup of \(G'\) if and only if \(H\) is one in \(G\). By definition, this question reduces to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. Now since \(N\) is central in \(G\), hence in \(H\), it follows that \(H\) is solvable if and only if \(H'\) is. Finally, taking into account the correspondence between algebraic subgroups of \(G'\) and algebraic subgroups of \(G\) containing \(N\), one sees at once that \(H'\) possesses the maximality property of definition 4.1 if and only if \(H\) possesses it, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.10

With the notations of 4.7, if \(B'\) and \(B\) are Borel subgroups of \(G'\) and \(G\) which correspond to each other, we have \[ \mathfrak{b} = \mathrm{Lie}(u)^{-1}(\mathfrak{b}') \] where \(\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{g}', \mathfrak{b}, \mathfrak{b}'\) are the Lie algebras of \(G, G', B, B'\), and where \(\mathrm{Lie}(u) : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}'\) is the homomorphism deduced from \(u\).
This statement follows trivially from the definitions and the relation \(u^{-1}(B') = B\).
We can now prove the main result of the present section:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.11

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra. Then \(\mathfrak{g}\) is equal to the union of the Lie algebras \(\mathfrak{b}\) of the Borel subgroups \(B\) of \(G\).

We may evidently assume \(G\) connected. Let \(R\) be the radical of \(G\) and let \(G' = G/R\). Then 4.9 b) and 4.10 reduce us to proving theorem 4.11 for \(G'\) instead of \(G\), i.e. we may assume \(G\) semi-simple. Then let \(Z\) be the center of \(G\), identical to the reductive center, and let \(G' = G/Z\). The same reasoning (now using 4.9 a)) reduces us to proving the theorem for \(G'\), i.e. we may assume \(G\) semi-simple adjoint. Let \(B\) be a Borel of \(G\), \(T\) a maximal torus of \(B\) hence of \(G\), and let \(\mathfrak{b}\) and \(\mathfrak{t}\) be the Lie algebras of \(B\) and \(T\). By virtue of 3.18, \(T\) is a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\), i.e. \(\mathfrak{t}\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), hence the union of the conjugates of \(\mathfrak{t}\) is dense in \(\mathfrak{g}\) (XIII 5.1 (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (vii)). A fortiori the union of the conjugates of \(\mathfrak{b}\) is dense in \(\mathfrak{g}\). Now let \(X\) be the closed subscheme of \(G/B \times \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) whose points with values in \(k\) are the \((g',x)\) such that \(x \in \mathrm{Ad}(g)\cdot\mathfrak{b}\) (cf. XIII 1). Then the morphism \(\psi : X \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) induced by the second projection is proper since \(G/B\) is proper over \(k\); on the other hand we have just seen that it is dominant, hence it is surjective, which proves 4.11.
The only result of the present exposé that we will use in the sequel of this exposé is the following corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.12

Let \(k\) be an infinite field, \(G\) a smooth algebraic group over \(k\), \(T\) a maximal torus of \(G\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) and \(\mathfrak{t}\) the Lie algebras, \(u : G \to \mathrm{GL}(V)\) a linear representation (\(V\) a finite-dimensional vector space over \(k\)), whence a representation of Lie algebras \(u' : \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{gl}(V)\), making \(V\) a \(\mathfrak{g}\)-module. Then the minimum of the nullity of \(u'(x)\) (\(x \in \mathfrak{g}\)) is attained for an element \(x \in \mathfrak{t}\).

One is reduced at once to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed. We may evidently assume \(G\) connected, and, dividing \(G\) by \((\mathrm{Ker}\,u)_{\mathrm{red}}\) if necessary, we may assume \(G\) affine. Using 4.11 and the conjugation theorem for the maximal tori of \(G\), we are reduced to the case where \(G\) is moreover solvable. \(G\) is a semi-direct product \(T \cdot V\), where \(V\) is the "unipotent part" of \(G\), which is a smooth connected unipotent group (BIBLE 6 th. 3). Hence \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the direct sum (as a vector space) of the Lie subalgebras \(\mathfrak{t}\) and \(\mathfrak{v} = \mathrm{Lie}(V)\). By virtue of the Lie-Kolchin theorem (BIBLE 6 th. 1), \(\mathfrak{v}\) admits a composition series by stable subspaces \(\mathfrak{v}_i\), such that \(\mathfrak{v}_i/\mathfrak{v}_{i+1} = \mathfrak{w}_i\) is of dimension 1. Then for each \(i\), we have an induced representation \(u_i : G \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{w}_i)\) and the corresponding Lie algebra homomorphism \(u_i' : \mathfrak{g} \to k\), so that for every \(x \in \mathfrak{g}\), the nullity of \(u'(x)\) is equal to the number of the \(i\) such that \(u_i'(x) = 0\). Since \(V\) is unipotent, the \(u_i\) are trivial on \(V\), hence the \(u_i'\) are trivial on \(\mathfrak{v}\), which proves that if \(x = t + v\) (\(t \in \mathfrak{t}\), \(v \in \mathfrak{v}\)), then \(u_i'(x) = u_i'(t)\) for all \(i\), hence the nullity of \(u'(x)\) is equal to that of \(u'(t)\). The assertion 4.12 follows at once.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: where \(\mathfrak{b}\) is the Lie algebra of \(B\).

pages.title: 5. Relations between Cartan subgroups and Cartan subalgebras

blocks.html: 5. Relations between Cartan subgroups and Cartan subalgebras
Applying 4.12 to the adjoint representation of \(G\), one finds:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over an infinite field, \(T\) a maximal torus of \(G\), \(\mathfrak{g} \supset \mathfrak{t}\) the Lie algebras; then \(\mathfrak{t}\) contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2
