Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation, \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra.

a) The conditions \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) to \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) of 2.9 on \(\mathfrak{g}\) are equivalent; in particular if the infinitesimal rank of the fibres of \(\mathfrak{g}\) at the points of \(S\) is locally constant, then \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits locally for the étale topology a Cartan subalgebra, hence (by 3.9 a)) \(G\) admits locally for the étale topology a subgroup of type (C).

b) Let \(H\) be a group subprescheme of \(G\) smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), with connected fibres having the same reductive rank as \(G\) at every \(s \in S\) (for example, \(H\) is a maximal torus or a Cartan subgroup of \(G\)), \(D\) a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\); then we have \(H \subset D\) if and only if we have \(\mathfrak{h} \subset \mathfrak{d}\).

c) Suppose condition \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is satisfied, i.e. the infinitesimal rank of \(G\) is locally constant. Let \(H\) be a group subprescheme of \(G\) smooth of finite presentation over \(S\), with connected nilpotent fibres, having the same reductive rank as \(G\) at every point (for example, \(H\) is a maximal torus or a Cartan subgroup of \(G\)). Then \(H\) is contained in one and only one subgroup \(D\) of type (C) of \(G\).

d) Suppose that \(G\) admits locally for fpqc a Cartan subgroup; then the same holds for every subgroup \(D\) of type (C) of \(G\).

Proof. a) Suppose condition \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is satisfied, and let us prove that the same holds for \((\mathrm{C}_2)\), i.e. that every quasi-regular section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is regular. One reduces as usual to the case \(S\) affine noetherian, then, the question being "infinitesimal" (2.15 b)), to the case where \(S\) is local artinian (then \((\mathrm{C}_0)\) is moreover trivially satisfied). We may further assume the residue field \(k\) of \(S\) infinite. Note that by virtue of 3.7 it suffices to establish that \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits a Cartan subalgebra. Let \(T\) be a maximal torus of \(G\); by virtue of 5.1 there exists a quasi-regular element \(t\) contained in the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{t}\) of \(T\); let us prove that it is regular, which will complete the proof. Consider the linear representation of \(T\) on \(\mathfrak{g}\) induced by the adjoint representation of \(G\); there exists then a finite set \((u_i)_{i \in I}\) of characters of \(T\), such that \(\mathfrak{g}\) decomposes into a direct sum of submodules \(\mathfrak{g}_i\) stable under \(T\), with \(T\) acting on \(\mathfrak{g}_i\) through \(u_i\) (cf. I §4.7.3). Let \(u_i' : \mathfrak{t} \to A\) be the Lie algebra homomorphism deduced from \(u_i : T \to \mathbb{G}_m\) (N.B. \(A\) denotes the ring of \(S\)). Consider the homomorphisms \(u_{i0}\) and \(u'_{i0}\) deduced from the preceding ones by passage to the fibres, i.e. by the base change \(A \to k\). Let \(I'\) be the set of \(i \in I\) such that \(u'_{i0} \neq 0\), and let \(I'' = I - I'\). The fact that \(t\) is regular is expressed by the condition \(u'_{i0}(t_0) \neq 0\) for all \(i \in I'\), hence \(u_i'(t)\) invertible for \(i \in I'\). The Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_0\) defined by \(t\), i.e. the kernel subspace of the semi-simple endomorphism \(\mathrm{ad}(t_0)\) of \(\mathfrak{g}_0\), is \(\sum_{i \in I''}(\mathfrak{g}_i)_0\). Consider \[ \mathfrak{d} = \sum_{i \in I''} \mathfrak{g}_i, \] then \(\mathrm{ad}(t)\) is nilpotent in \(\mathfrak{d}\); on the other hand it is an automorphism of \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d} \simeq \sum_{i \in I'} \mathfrak{g}_i\). By virtue of 2.6 \(t\) is therefore regular.
b) Follows from 3.12 a), \(H\) satisfying the hypothesis that every geometric fibre of \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a regular element of that of \(\mathfrak{g}\), thanks to 5.1.

c) By virtue of b), we are reduced to proving that \(\mathfrak{h}\) is contained in one and only one Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). By virtue of a) we know moreover that \(\mathfrak{g}\) satisfies \((\mathrm{C}_2)\). We are thus reduced to the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.3

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be a Lie algebra over \(S\) which is a locally free module of finite type, and satisfies condition \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) of 2.9. Let \(\mathfrak{h}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) which satisfies the following conditions: it is a module which is locally a direct factor, it is locally nilpotent, and for every \(s \in S\), the geometric fibre \(\mathfrak{h}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\overline{s}}\). Then \(\mathfrak{h}\) is contained in one and only one Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

(N.B. in the case of interest to us, \(\mathfrak{h}\) satisfies the stated conditions: it is locally nilpotent because \(H\) has nilpotent fibres, and the condition on regular elements follows from 5.1). Since 5.3 is local for the fpqc topology, it suffices to prove that at a point \(s \in S\) such that \(\kappa(s)\) is infinite, there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that existence and uniqueness hold for every base change \(S' \to S\) factoring through \(U\). Take a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g} \otimes \kappa(s)\) contained in \(\mathfrak{h} \otimes \kappa(s)\), extend it to a section \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{h}\) over an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\); thanks to \((\mathrm{C}_2)\) we may assume that this section is regular (2.10). We may assume \(U = S\). A Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) which contains \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains \(a\), hence is identical to \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) (2.6), whence the uniqueness. Moreover, since \(\mathfrak{h}\) is locally nilpotent, we have \(\mathfrak{h} \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) (2.1), whence the existence.
d) This is a special case of XII 7.9 d).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Let \(G\) be a smooth and connected algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(H\) a connected algebraic subgroup such that \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), i.e. such that \(H\) contains a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\). Then the number of conjugates of \(H\) containing a regular element of \(G(k)\) is equal to the number of conjugates of \(\mathfrak{h}\) containing a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

Indeed, let \(g\) be a regular element of \(G(k)\), \(C\) the unique Cartan subgroup of \(G\) containing \(g\) (XIII 2); then the conjugates of \(H\) containing \(g\) are those containing \(C\) (XIII 2.8 b)). Likewise let \(x\) be a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\); then if a conjugate \(\mathfrak{h}'\) of \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains \(x\), then \(\mathrm{ad}(x)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}'}\) is injective (XIII 5.4) hence \(\mathfrak{h}'\) contains \(\mathrm{Nil}(x,\mathfrak{g}) = \mathfrak{d}\), hence the number of conjugates of \(\mathfrak{h}\) containing \(x\) is equal to the number of conjugates containing the Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\). Moreover \(\mathfrak{d}\) is the Lie algebra of a subgroup \(D\) of type (C) of \(G\). We may evidently assume \(C \subset D\), and the assertion 5.4 will follow from this: for \(H\) to contain \(C\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak{h}\) contain \(\mathfrak{d}\). Indeed, by virtue of XIII 5.5 the relation \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{d}\) implies \(H \supset D\) and a fortiori \(H \supset C\). Conversely, by 5.1 \(\mathfrak{t}\) contains a regular element \(x\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\); then \(H \supset C\) implies \(\mathfrak{h} \ni x\), hence, as we have already pointed out, this implies \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{d}\). This completes the proof.

Let \(G\) be as in 5.2 and suppose that the infinitesimal rank of the fibres of \(G\) remains locally constant (condition \((\mathrm{C}_0)\)). Then thanks to 5.2 c), one finds a homomorphism of functors on \((\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S}\): \[ \mathscr{C} \longrightarrow \mathscr{D} \] where \[ \mathscr{C}(S') = \text{set of Cartan subgroups of } G_{S'} \] \[ \mathscr{D}(S') = \text{set of subgroups of type (C) of } G_{S'}. \]

By virtue of 3.10 and 5.2 a), \(\mathscr{D}\) is representable by a prescheme smooth and quasi-projective over \(S\). Consider the subgroup \(D\) of type (C) of \(G_{\mathscr{D}}\) playing the universal role with respect to \(G/S\); one can then consider the functor \(\mathscr{C}_D : (\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathscr{D}} \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) defined in terms of the \(\mathscr{D}\)-group \(D\) as \(\mathscr{C}\) is in terms of the \(S\)-group \(G\). We then have the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.5

Under the preceding conditions, consider \(\mathscr{C}\) as a functor over the prescheme \(\mathscr{D}\); then \(\mathscr{C}\) is \(\mathscr{D}\)-isomorphic to the functor \(\mathscr{C}_D\) "of Cartan subgroups of \(D\)".
This follows at once from the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation, \(D\) a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\). Then there is a one-to-one correspondence between the Cartan subgroups of \(G\) contained in \(D\) and the Cartan subgroups of \(D\) (precisely, for a subgroup \(H\) of \(G\), \(H\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(G\) contained in \(D\) if and only if it is a Cartan subgroup of \(D\)).
Indeed, this is a special case of XII 7.9 c), taking into account that over an algebraically closed field, a subgroup of type (C) of \(G\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(G\).
For the next section, the main result obtained here is 5.2 c) for \(H\) a Cartan subgroup of \(G\), which makes it possible to state 5.5 and thus provides a useful "dévissage" of \(\mathscr{C}\).

pages.title: 6. Applications to the structure of algebraic groups

blocks.html: 6. Applications to the structure of algebraic groups

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.1

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\). Consider the scheme \(\mathscr{T}\) of maximal tori of \(G\), isomorphic to the scheme \(\mathscr{C}\) of Cartan subgroups of \(G\), which is a homogeneous space under \(G^0\), and a smooth affine connected algebraic scheme (XII 7.1 d)). Then \(\mathscr{C}\) is a rational variety, i.e. the field of rational functions of \(\mathscr{C}\) is a purely transcendental extension of \(k\).

We will first give the proof in the case where \(k\) is infinite. We may evidently assume \(G\) connected, for \(\mathscr{T}\), hence \(\mathscr{C}\), does not change upon replacing \(G\) by \(G^0\). Moreover, by virtue of XII 7.6, \(\mathscr{C}\) does not change upon dividing \(G\) by a central subgroup. This allows us, dividing first by the center of \(G\), to assume \(G\) affine (XII 6.1), then, dividing by its reductive center (XII 4.1 and 4.4), to assume that the reductive center of \(G\) is trivial (XII 4.7 b)). Furthermore we proceed by induction on \(n = \dim G\), assuming the theorem proved for dimensions \(n' < n\). If \(G\) is nilpotent, then \(\mathscr{C}\) is reduced to a point rational over \(k\), and 6.1 is trivial. Otherwise, the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) of \(G\) is non-nilpotent (1.3), hence the Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) are of dimension \(n' < n\), hence the subgroups of type (C) of \(G\) are of dimension \(n' < n\). Consider then the morphism \[ \mathscr{C} \longrightarrow \mathscr{D} \] considered in 5.5. We know by 3.10 (\(k\) being an infinite field, hence \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing a regular element) that \(\mathscr{D}\) is a rational variety, i.e. the field \(K\) of rational functions on \(\mathscr{D}\) is a purely transcendental extension of \(k\). Consider the fibre of \(\mathscr{C}\) at the generic point \(x\) of \(\mathscr{D}\); by virtue of 5.5 it is the scheme of Cartan subgroups of a certain algebraic group \(D_x\) smooth and connected over \(K = \kappa(x)\) (namely \(D_x = \)"the generic subgroup of type (C) of \(G\)"). The field \(L\) of rational functions on \(\mathscr{C}\) is therefore isomorphic to the field of rational functions on \(\mathscr{C}_{D_x}\), which, by the induction hypothesis (since \(\dim D_x = n' < n\)) is a purely transcendental extension of \(K\). Hence by transitivity \(L\) is a purely transcendental extension of \(k\).

When \(k\) is finite, a different proof is needed. We may again assume that \(G\) is affine and connected. Note that \(k\) is perfect; it follows at once that the radical \(\overline{R}\) of \(G_{\overline{k}}\) is "defined over \(k\)", i.e. comes from a subgroup \(R\) of \(G\). Suppose first \(R \neq G\), i.e. \(G\) not solvable, and let \[ u : G \longrightarrow G' = G/R \] be the canonical morphism. Consider the corresponding morphism \(C \mapsto u(C)\) \[ v : \mathscr{C}_G \longrightarrow \mathscr{C}_{G'} \] (whose definition is immediate by virtue of XII 7.1 e)). Let \(x\) be the generic point of \(\mathscr{C}_{G'}\); then the fibre \(v^{-1}(x)\) is identified with the scheme of Cartan subgroups of \(G_K\) (where \(K = \kappa(x)\)) whose image in \(G'_K\) is a certain Cartan subgroup \(C'_x\) (namely "the generic Cartan subgroup of \(G'\)"). It is therefore also the scheme of Cartan subgroups of \(H = u_K^{-1}(C')\) (XII 7.9 c)), and since \(K\) is here an infinite extension of \(k\), it follows from the part of 7.1 already proved that the field \(L\) of rational functions of \(\mathscr{C}_G\), equal to that of \(\mathscr{C}_H\), is a purely transcendental extension of \(K\). To prove that it is also a purely transcendental extension of \(k\), it therefore suffices to prove that \(K\) is one, i.e. it suffices to prove the theorem in the case where \(G\) is semi-simple. We may moreover assume that \(G\) is adjoint (dividing \(G\) by its reductive center if necessary). But then by virtue of 3.18 we have \(\mathscr{C}_G \simeq \mathscr{D}_G\), and by virtue of 3.10 it suffices to prove that \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits a regular point, which (as was pointed out in 3.20) is an unpublished result of Chevalley10.
It thus remains only to treat the case where \(k\) is finite, \(G\) connected affine solvable. We have in fact a more general result:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2

Let \(G\) be a smooth solvable algebraic group over a field \(k\); then the variety \(\mathscr{C}\) of Cartan subgroups of \(G\) is isomorphic to an affine space \(\mathrm{Spec}\,k[t_1,\ldots,t_{\mathrm{N}}]\).

We may again assume \(G\) connected, and affine. Let \(G_{\infty}\) be the smallest of the groups occurring in the descending central series of \(G\) (by \(G_i\) such that \(G_{i+1} = [G,G_i]\)); it is thus the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of \(G\) such that \(G/G_{\infty}\) is nilpotent. Let \(C\) be a Cartan subgroup of \(G\) (one exists by virtue of 1.1); then the image of \(C\) in \(G/G_{\infty}\) is a Cartan subgroup of it, hence is equal to \(G/G_{\infty}\); consequently the morphism \(g \mapsto \mathrm{Ad}(g)\cdot C\) from \(G_{\infty}\) to \(\mathscr{C}\) is an epimorphism, and identifies \(\mathscr{C}\) with the homogeneous space \(G_{\infty}/N \cap G_{\infty}\), where \(N\) is the normalizer of \(C\) in \(G\) (moreover equal to \(C\) as was recalled in 4.4, but that does not matter here). Note that \(U = G_{\infty}\) is evidently a smooth connected "unipotent" algebraic group (for over the algebraic closure of \(k\), it is contained in the unipotent part of \(G\), by virtue of the known structure of smooth affine solvable groups, BIBLE 6 th. 3). When \(k\) is perfect (the only case needed to establish 6.1), it follows very easily that \(U\) is even \(k\)-unipotent, i.e. admits a composition series by algebraic subgroups \(U_i\) such that \(U_i/U_{i+1}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). In fact, Rosenlicht proved that this result remains valid for a group of the form \(G_{\infty}\) as above, without restriction on \(k\) (M. Rosenlicht, Questions of rationality for solvable algebraic groups over non perfect fields, Annali di Matematica 1963, pp. 97-120, theorem 4 cor. 2), a distinctly more delicate result which we will take for granted here. It now suffices to apply the following lemma, no doubt well known to the specialists:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3

Let \(U\) be a smooth connected algebraic group over a field \(k\), \(X = U/V\) a homogeneous space under \(U\) having a rational point over \(k\). Suppose \(U\) is \(k\)-unipotent. Then as a \(k\)-scheme, \(X\) is isomorphic to an affine space \(\mathrm{Spec}\,k[t_1,\ldots,t_{\mathrm{N}}]\).

Indeed, let \((U_i)_{0 \leqslant i \leqslant n}\) be a composition series of \(U\) by smooth connected subgroups, with \(U_n = (e)\), \(U_0 = U\), \(U_i/U_{i+1} \simeq \mathbb{G}_a\), the \(U_i\) normal in \(U\). Then the \(K_i = U_i V\) are algebraic subgroups of \(U\) (not necessarily smooth nor connected if \(V\) is not, but that matters little to us), and \(K_{i+1}\) is normal in \(K_i\). We have a canonical morphism \(U_i/U_{i+1} \to K_i/K_{i+1}\) which is an epimorphism, which proves that \(K_i/K_{i+1}\) is either reduced to the unit group, or isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a/H_i\), where \(H_i\) is a finite subgroup of \(\mathbb{G}_a\), which by virtue of Rosenlicht (loc. cit., th 2) implies that \(K_i/K_{i+1} \simeq \mathbb{G}_a\) (a result which is moreover immediate if \(k\) is perfect). Now set \(X_i = U/K_i\); let us prove by induction on \(i\) that \(X_i\) is isomorphic to an affine space. Indeed, if it is so for \(X_i\), let us prove that the same holds for \(X_{i+1}\). If \(K_i/K_{i+1} = e\) we have \(X_i = X_{i+1}\) and it is trivial. Otherwise, \(X_{i+1}\) is a principal bundle with base \(X_i\) and structure group \(\mathbb{G}_a \simeq K_i/K_{i+1}\). Hence, \(X_i\) being affine, \(X_{i+1}\) is a trivial bundle, hence is isomorphic to \(X_i \times \mathbb{G}_a\), which again proves that \(X_{i+1}\) is isomorphic to a standard affine space. This proves 6.2 and consequently completes the proof of 6.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.4

Let \(G\) be a smooth algebraic group over an infinite field \(k\). Then the set of points of \(\mathscr{C}\) (notations of 6.1) rational over \(k\) is dense for the Zariski topology. The union of the Cartan subgroups of \(G\) is dense in \(G\).

The first assertion is valid for every unirational variety over an infinite field, and is moreover here for us the most important "arithmetic" consequence of the unirationality results. The second assertion follows from the first and from the density theorem XIII 2.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.5

Let \(G\) be a smooth connected algebraic group over \(k\). Then the variety \(Z\) of regular semi-simple points of \(G\) (XIII 3.5) is a unirational variety. In particular, if \(k\) is infinite, the set of points of \(Z\) rational over \(k\) is dense in \(Z\).

Indeed, \(Z\) is an open subscheme of a torus over \(\mathscr{C}\), hence its function field \(L\) is the function field of a torus defined over the function field \(K\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) (namely the "generic maximal torus" of \(G\)); it is therefore a unirational extension of \(K\) (XIII 3.4), and since \(K\) is a purely transcendental extension of \(k\) by virtue of 6.1, \(L\) is a unirational extension of \(k\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6

Let \(G\) be a smooth connected algebraic group over \(k\), and let \(H\) be the subgroup of \(G\) generated by the subvariety \(Z\) of regular semi-simple points, i.e. (XII 8.2) the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of \(G\) such that \(G/H\) is of reductive rank zero (i.e. is, over the algebraic closure of \(k\), an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected unipotent group). Then \(H\) is a unirational variety. In particular, if \(G = H\), i.e. (XII 8.4) if \(G\) is affine and if over the algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) there is no non-trivial homomorphism from \(G_{\overline{k}}\) to \(\mathbb{G}_a\), then \(G\) is a unirational variety, hence if \(k\) is infinite, the set of its points rational over \(k\) is dense.

This follows at once from 6.5, for it is immediate that if one has smooth connected \(k\)-preschemas \(Z_i\) which are unirational varieties and morphisms \(u_i : Z_i \to G\), then the algebraic subgroup of \(G\) generated by the \(u_i\) (VIB 7.1) is a unirational variety.
As an interesting special case of 6.5 or 6.6 (as one prefers), let us note:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.7

Let \(G\) be a smooth connected affine algebraic group of unipotent rank zero; then \(G\) is a unirational variety.
One can refine 6.4 in the following way:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.8

Let \(G\) be a smooth connected algebraic group over the infinite field \(k\), and let \(C\) be a Cartan subgroup of \(G\). Then the union of the conjugates of \(C\) by regular semi-simple elements of \(G(k)\) is dense in \(G\).

This follows at once from 6.4 and from XIII 3.6 which says that the morphism \(\varphi : Z \times C \to G\) defined by \(\varphi(t,c) = \mathrm{ad}(t)c\) is dominant. This result also implies (without supposing \(k\) infinite):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.9

Let \(G\) be a smooth connected algebraic group over \(k\), \(H\) a smooth connected algebraic subgroup of \(G\), such that \(H\) has the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank as \(G\) (i.e. over the algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\), \(H_{\overline{k}}\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(G_{\overline{k}}\)). If \(H\) is a unirational variety, so is \(G\). If \(H(k)\) is dense in \(H\), \(G(k)\) is dense in \(G\).

Indeed, the morphism \(\varphi : Z \times H \to G\) defined by \(\varphi(t,h) = \mathrm{ad}(t)h\) is dominant; now by virtue of 6.5, \(Z\) is a unirational variety, and by hypothesis so is \(H\), hence so is \(Z \times H\), whence the first result. The second is proved in an analogous way.
We now recover the following well-known result (due to Chevalley, in characteristic 0, and to Rosenlicht in characteristic \(p > 0\)):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.10

Let \(G\) be a smooth connected affine algebraic group over a perfect field \(k\). Then \(G\) is a unirational variety, hence if \(k\) is moreover infinite, \(G(k)\) is dense in \(G\).

Indeed, by virtue of 1.1, \(G\) admits a Cartan subgroup \(C\). By virtue of 6.9 it suffices to prove that the latter is a unirational variety. Now \(k\) being perfect, one sees at once by Galois descent from the case \(k\) algebraically closed (BIBLE 6 th. 2) that we have \(C = T \times C_u\), where \(T\) is the maximal torus of \(C\) and \(C_u\) a smooth connected unipotent group. We already know that \(T\) is a unirational variety (XIII 3.4); it remains to see that the same holds for \(C_u\). Now \(k\) being perfect, \(C_u\) is even \(k\)-unipotent, and one can apply 6.3.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 6.11

a) One knows (Rosenlicht) examples of twisted forms of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) over a non-perfect field which have only a finite number of rational points, hence a fortiori are not unirational varieties. On the other hand, Chevalley gave an example of a torus over a field of characteristic zero which is not a rational variety. By contrast, it follows from Chevalley's theory of semi-simple groups that over an algebraically closed field, every smooth connected affine algebraic group is a rational variety. Note moreover that the question of unirationality arises in any case only for affine algebraic groups, a unirational algebraic group being necessarily affine by Chevalley's structure theorem.

b) With the notations of 6.6, it is tempting to try to give a condition for unirationality of \(G\) in terms of the group \(G/H\) (which is unipotent if \(G\) is assumed affine). It is evidently necessary that the latter be unirational; is this condition also sufficient? Note that an example of Rosenlicht (loc. cit.) shows that a smooth connected unipotent algebraic group \(U\) can be a rational variety without being \(k\)-unipotent.

c) It would be interesting to study, over a finite field \(k\), questions of "density" type, such as the following (raised by Rosenlicht): Let \(G\) be a smooth and connected algebraic group over \(k\); is \(G\) then generated by its Cartan subgroups1112?

In this question, one can reduce to the case \(G\) affine, by dividing by the center. The answer would be affirmative in the case \(G\) semi-simple, if one could sharpen Chevalley's result on the existence of regular points mentioned in 3.20, so as to obtain a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\) which does not belong to \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathrm{Lie}(H)\), where \(H\) is a smooth algebraic subgroup of \(G\), \(\neq G\), given in advance (\(G\) an adjoint semi-simple group over the finite field \(k\)).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: cf. the Appendix, by J.-P. Serre.
This question has since been answered in the affirmative by Steinberg.

N.D.E.: not having identified this result in the Collected Papers of R. Steinberg, let us give a proof based on the Bruhat decomposition. Let \(G/k\) be a semi-simple group defined over the finite field \(k\). The point is to show that \(G\) is generated by its Cartan \(k\)-subgroups; one is reduced, by central isogeny and by restriction of scalars, to the case where \(G\) is geometrically simple (in the same way as in Lemma 1 of the appendix below). Let \(G^{\sharp}\) denote the subgroup of \(G\) generated by its \(k\)-subtori. The group \(G\) is quasi-split (Lang) and therefore admits a Killing pair \((T,B)\). By definition, we have \(T \subset G^{\sharp}\); in particular \(T\) normalizes \(G^{\sharp}\). The big cell \(\mathrm{R}_u(B^-)\,T\,\mathrm{R}_u(B)\) of \(G\) shows that it suffices to check that \(\mathrm{R}_u(B) \subset G^{\sharp}\). Let \(S\) be the maximal split \(k\)-torus of \(T\). Given \(\alpha \in \Phi(G,S)\), let \(\mathrm{U}_{(\alpha)}\) denote the unipotent subgroup associated with \(\alpha\) (cf. A. Borel, Linear Algebraic Groups, second edition (1991), Springer, Prop. 21.9). Since the \(k\)-group \(\mathrm{R}_u(B)\) is generated by the unipotent \(k\)-groups \(\mathrm{U}_{(\alpha)}\) (\(\alpha \in \Delta_k\)), one is reduced to checking that \(\mathrm{U}_{(\alpha)} \subset G_{\alpha} \subset G\). One may assume that \(G = \mathrm{PGL}_2\) or \(G = \mathrm{SU}_3(K)\), where \(K\) denotes the unique quadratic field extension of \(k\). The group \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp}\) containing the standard split torus \(T\), the possibilities (up to conjugation) under \(G(k)\) are the following: \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = T\), \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = B\), or \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = \mathrm{PGL}_2\). The case \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = T\) is excluded since \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp}\) contains the \(k\)-torus \(\mathrm{R}^1_{K/k}(\mathbb{G}_m)/\mathbb{G}_m\). The preceding discussion shows that if \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = B\), then \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = \mathrm{PGL}_2\). We therefore have \(\mathrm{PGL}_2^{\sharp} = \mathrm{PGL}_2\). If \(G = \mathrm{SU}_3(K)\), \(\Delta_k = \{\alpha\}\) and the possibilities for \(G^{\sharp}\) (up to conjugation) are the following: \(G^{\sharp} = T\), \(G^{\sharp} = B\), \(G^{\sharp} = \mathrm{U}_{(2\alpha)} \rtimes T\), \(G^{\sharp} = \langle \mathrm{U}_{(2\alpha)}, \mathrm{U}_{(-2\alpha)}, T\rangle = \mathrm{SL}_2 \cdot T\), or \(G^{\sharp} = G\). The case \(G^{\sharp} = B\) is excluded as for \(\mathrm{PGL}_2\). The case \(\mathrm{SL}_2 \cdot T\) is excluded because \(G^{\sharp}\) contains the \(k\)-torus \(\mathrm{R}^1_{K/k}\!\left(\mathrm{R}^1_{K_3/K}(\mathbb{G}_m)\right)\), \(K_3/K\) denoting the extension of degree 3 of \(K\), and the isogeny class of this torus is irreducible. Furthermore, this torus also excludes the cases \(G^{\sharp} = T\) and \(G^{\sharp} = \mathrm{U}_{(2\alpha)} \rtimes T\). One concludes that \(G^{\sharp} = G\).

pages.title: 7. Appendix: Existence of regular elements over finite fields, by J.-P. Serre

blocks.html:
7. Appendix: Existence of regular elements over finite fields
by J.-P. Serre

In all that follows, \(k\) denotes a finite field, and \(\overline{k}\) its algebraic closure; the Galois group of \(\overline{k}/k\) is denoted \(\mathscr{G}\); recall that, if \(q = \mathrm{Card}(k)\), \(\mathscr{G}\) is topologically generated by the "Frobenius" element \(\mathrm{F} : x \mapsto x^q\).
We propose to prove the following theorem13:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem

Let \(G\) be an adjoint semi-simple group defined over \(k\), and let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be its Lie algebra. The \(k\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}(k)\) contains a regular element.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks

(1) It is worth recalling that "adjoint" means that the center of \(G\) is trivial (as a group subscheme of \(G\)). In view of the Chevalley seminar, this also means that, if \(T\) is a maximal torus of \(G\), the group \(X(T)\) of characters of \(T\) (defined over \(\overline{k}\)) is generated by the set \(R\) of roots. It follows in particular that the rank of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) is equal to the dimension of \(T\) (i.e. to the rank of \(G\)).

(2) The writer does not know whether the hypothesis that \(G\) is adjoint is indispensable.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1

It suffices to prove the theorem when \(G\) is geometrically simple.

(We say that \(G\) is geometrically simple if \(G \otimes \overline{k}\) contains no normal group subscheme smooth over \(\overline{k}\), apart from \(G\) and \(\{e\}\); an equivalent condition: the associated root system \(R\) is irreducible).

We may first assume \(G\) is indecomposable over \(k\). The group \(G \otimes \overline{k}\) is then a product of geometrically simple components \(S\) which are permuted transitively by the Galois group \(\mathscr{G}\). If \(\mathscr{H}\) is the subgroup of \(\mathscr{G}\) leaving one of these components \(S\) fixed, this component is defined over the field \(K\) corresponding to \(\mathscr{H}\) (i.e. comes by extension of scalars from a subscheme of \(G \otimes K\)), and a standard argument shows that \(G = \prod_{K/k}(S)\) (i.e. \(\mathrm{R}_{K/k}(S)\), for readers used to Weil's notations). Likewise, the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) of \(G\) is identified with \(\prod_{K/k} \mathfrak{s}\), where \(\mathfrak{s}\) is the Lie algebra of \(S\). If the theorem is true for \(S\), there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{s}(K) = \mathfrak{g}(k)\) which is regular in \(\mathfrak{s}\); one then verifies easily that it is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\). Q.E.D.

From now on, we assume \(G\) is geometrically simple and we choose a maximal torus \(T\) of \(G\) (we know that this is possible). We denote by \(X\) the group of characters of \(T \otimes \overline{k}\), \(R\) its root system, \(W\) its Weyl group and \(E\) the group of automorphisms of \(X\) preserving \(R\). The group \(W\) is a normal subgroup of \(E\).

If \(T'\) is another torus of \(G\), we denote by \(X'\), \(R'\), \(W'\), \(E'\) the corresponding character group, root system,…. If one chooses \(y \in G(\overline{k})\) such that \(y \cdot (T \otimes \overline{k}) \cdot y^{-1} = T' \otimes \overline{k}\), one can identify \(X'\), \(R'\), \(W'\), \(E'\) with \(X, R, W, E\) thanks to \(\mathrm{int}(y)\). Changing \(y\) modifies this identification by an automorphism of \(X\) corresponding to an element of \(W\).

The canonical generator \(x \mapsto x^q\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) acts on \(X'\) leaving \(R'\) stable; it therefore defines an element \(f_{T'}\) of \(E'\). We set in particular \(f = f_T\). When \(E'\) is identified with \(E\) as just described, the element \(f_{T'}\) is transformed into an element \(f'\) of \(E\), defined up to replacement by \(wf'w^{-1}\), with \(w \in W\). We are going to compare this element with \(f\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2

We have \(f' \equiv f \mod W\); conversely, every \(f' \in E\) satisfying this condition can be obtained from a maximal torus \(T'\) of \(G\).

Set \(\overline{T} = T \otimes \overline{k}\), \(\overline{T}' = T' \otimes \overline{k}\), and let \(y \in G(\overline{k})\) be such that \(y\overline{T}y^{-1} = \overline{T}'\); since \(y^q\overline{T}y^{-q} = \overline{T}'\), one concludes that \(n = y^{-1}y^q\) belongs to the normalizer \(\mathrm{N}(T)\) of \(T\). The effect of \(f'\) on the points of \(T(\overline{k})\) is then the following: \[ f'(t) = y^{-1}(yty^{-1})^q y = y^{-1}y^q t^q y^{-q} y = n t^q n^{-1}. \] If \(w \in W\) is the element defined by \(n\), this shows that \(f'\) and \(f \circ w\) have the same effect on the points of \(\overline{T}\), hence also on its characters, and we have \(f' = f \circ w\), whence \(f' \equiv f \mod W\).

Conversely, if \(w \in W\) is given, one represents it by an element \(n \in \mathrm{N}(T)(\overline{k})\); thanks to a classical theorem of Lang, one can write \(n\) in the form \(n = y^{-1}y^q\), with \(y \in G(\overline{k})\); the torus \(T' = yTy^{-1}\) is then defined over \(k\), and the preceding computation shows that the corresponding \(f'\) is equal to \(f \circ w\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3

Let \(X, R, W, E\) be as above (\(R\) being irreducible), and let \(\varphi \in E/W\). There then exist an element \(f \in E\) representing \(\varphi\), as well as a family \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\) of roots enjoying the two following properties:

(1) \((\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\) is a basis of \(X\).

(2) \(R\) is the union of the orbits of the \(\theta_i\) under the powers of \(f\) (i.e. every \(a \in R\) can be written \(a = f^m\theta_i\), with suitable \(m\) and \(i\)).
The proof will be given a little further on.
Here now is a lemma of linear algebra:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4

Let \(V\) be a vector space over \(k\), and let \((\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\) be a basis of the dual of \(V \otimes \overline{k}\). There exists \(x \in V\) such that \(\theta_i(x) \neq 0\) for all \(i\).

Let \(V^*\) be the dual of \(V\), and let \(\overline{W}_i\) be the subspace of \(V^* \otimes \overline{k}\) generated by the conjugates of \(\theta_i\); this subspace is defined over \(k\), i.e. of the form \(W_i \otimes \overline{k}\) where \(W_i \subset V^*\). The evident map \(W_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes W_n \to \bigwedge^n V^*\) is not zero (otherwise its extension to \(\overline{k}\) would be zero as well, which is absurd since \(\theta_i \in W_i \otimes \overline{k}\), and \(\theta_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \theta_n \neq 0\)). There exists therefore a basis of \(V^*\) formed of elements \(u_i \in W_i\). Let \(x \in V\) be such that \(u_i(x) \neq 0\) for all \(i\) (for example \(u_i(x) = 1\)). The element \(x\) answers the question, for, if one had \(\theta_i(x) = 0\), the conjugates of \(\theta_i\) would also vanish at \(x\), and the same would hold for \(u_i\), which is not the case.
End of the proof of the theorem.

By combining lemmas 2 and 3, one can choose a torus \(T\) whose element \(f\) satisfies the properties of lemma 3. If \(Y\) is the dual of \(X\), the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{t}(\overline{k})\) of \(\overline{T}\)14 is canonically identified with \(Y \otimes \overline{k}\), and this operation is compatible with the action of the Galois group (the latter acting on \(Y \otimes \overline{k}\) through its action on \(Y\) and on \(\overline{k}\)). Let \(V = \mathfrak{t}(k)\) be the Lie algebra of \(T\) over \(k\). An element \(x \in V\) is regular if and only if it is annihilated by no root \(\alpha \in R\), or rather by none of the linear forms \(\overline{\alpha} \in V^* \otimes \overline{k}\) canonically defined by the \(\alpha \in R\). By lemma 4, one can find such an \(x\) which is annihilated by none of the roots \(\theta_i\); but every root is a conjugate of some \(\theta_i\) (this is what condition (2) of lemma 3 expresses); it follows that \(x\) is annihilated by no root, and it is indeed a regular element.
Proof of lemma 3.
It relies on the properties of Coxeter transformations. Let us briefly recall what this consists of:

Let \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) be a simple system of roots of \(R\), and, for every \(i\), let \(r_i\) be the symmetry corresponding to \(a_i\). Set: \[ c = r_1 \cdots r_n. \] We have \(c \in W\); of course the element \(c\) depends on the choice of the simple system \((a_1,\ldots,a_n)\) as well as on the order of the \(a_i\); however one shows that its conjugacy class depends on none of these choices. One calls \(c\) the Coxeter element of the system considered. One proves (we will take it for granted) that \(c\) does not admit 1 as an eigenvalue.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5

Set \(\theta_i = r_n \cdots r_{i+1}(a_i)\). Then:

(a) The \(\theta_i\) form a basis of the group \(X\) generated by the roots,

(b) We have \(\theta_i > 0\) and \(c(\theta_i) < 0\) for all \(i\).15

(c) Every root \(a \in R\) such that \(a > 0\) and \(c(a) < 0\) is equal to one of the \(\theta_i\).

(d) \(R\) is the union of the orbits of the \(\theta_i\) under the powers of \(c\).

It is clear that \(\theta_i\) is of the form \(a_i + \sum_{j>i} m_{ij}a_j\), with \(m_{ij} \in \mathbb{Z}\), which proves (a). Assertions (b) and (c) are consequences of the following remark: the symmetry \(r_i\) preserves the sign of every root distinct from \(\pm a_i\), and changes the sign of \(\pm a_i\). Finally, for (d) one remarks that an orbit of \(c\) cannot be formed entirely of positive (resp. negative) roots, for, by taking the sum of these roots one would find a non-zero element of \(X\) invariant under \(c\), and we have agreed to grant that \(c\) does not admit 1 as an eigenvalue. There is therefore necessarily in every orbit an element \(a > 0\) such that \(c(a) < 0\), and one applies (c).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

We have sketched the preceding proof only to make the reader's task easier; we could have confined ourselves to referring to the canonical texts on Coxeter (cf. for example Koszul, Séminaire Bourbaki, 1959/1960, exposé 191). Said texts contain other results: the orbits of \(c\) all have the same number \(h\) of elements, and each contains only one \(\theta_i\). In particular \(h = \mathrm{Card}(R)/n\).
Let us now return to the proof of lemma 3. We distinguish three cases:
(1) The given element \(\varphi \in E/W\) is equal to the identity element.
One must then take \(f \in W\); we choose \(f = c\); this works by lemma 5.
(2) The system \(R\) is of type \(\mathrm{A}_n\), with \(n\) even \(\geqslant 2\), and \(\varphi\) is the unique non-trivial element of \(E/W\).

We know that \(-1 \notin W\); we then take \(f = -c\). A simple computation shows that \(c\) is of order \(h = n+1\); its order is therefore odd. If \(a \in R\) is an arbitrary root, we have \(a = c^m\theta_i\) for some pair \((m,i)\), cf. lemma 5; adding \(h\) to \(m\) if necessary, we may assume \(m\) even, and one sees that we then have \(a = (-c)^m\theta_i = f^m\theta_i\). The orbits of the \(\theta_i\) therefore indeed fill up \(R\).
(3) The element \(\varphi \in E/W\) is non-trivial, and \(R\) is of one of the following types:
\(\mathrm{A}_n\), \(n\) odd \(\geqslant 3\)
\[ \xymatrix{ \bullet \ar@{-}[r] \ar@/^1.6pc/[rrrr] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] \ar@/^1.3pc/[rr] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] & \bullet } \]
\[ \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar@{-}[d] & \\ \bullet \ar@{-}[r] \ar@/^1.3pc/[rr] & \bullet & \bullet \ar@{-}[l] } \]
\(\mathrm{D}_n\), \(n \geqslant 5\)
\[ \xymatrix{ & & & \bullet & \\ \bullet \ar@{-}[r] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] & \bullet \ar@{-}[u] \ar@{-}[d] & \\ & & & \bullet \ar@/_1.4pc/[uu] & } \]
\[ \xymatrix{ \bullet \ar@{-}[r] \ar@/^1.6pc/[rrrr] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] \ar@/^1.3pc/[rr] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] \ar@{-}[d] & \bullet \ar@{-}[r] & \bullet \\ & & \bullet & & } \]
(A glance at the classification shows that these are indeed all the cases (together with \(\mathrm{A}_n\), \(n\) even) where \(E/W\) is non-trivial.)

Let \(S\) be a simple system of roots, which we do not number for the moment. We know that \(E\) is the semi-direct product of \(W\) and the group \(\Psi\) of permutations of \(S\) which leave the Cartan matrix invariant (or the Dynkin diagram, which is the same thing). The group \(E/W\) is thus identified with \(\Psi\), and in particular \(\varphi\) corresponds to an element \(\psi \in \Psi\). One observes by inspection of the Dynkin diagrams (cf. figures above) that every orbit of \(\psi\) in \(S\) is formed of pairwise orthogonal roots (i.e. not linked in the diagram). [Note that this would not be the case for \(\mathrm{A}_n\) (\(n\) even), which is what forced us to treat that case separately.] If \(\sigma\) is such an orbit, the symmetries \(r_a\), \(a \in \sigma\), commute with one another; their product will be denoted \(\rho_{\sigma}\). It is clear that \(\rho_{\sigma}\) commutes with \(\psi\).

This being so, let us choose on \(S\) a total order such that every orbit is a segment for this order relation; this amounts to numbering the elements of \(S\): \(S = \{a_1,\ldots,a_n\}\). Let \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\) be the roots defined above, and \(c = r_1 \cdots r_n\) the corresponding Coxeter element. The element \(c\) is the product of the \(\rho_{\sigma}\), the \(\sigma\) being arranged in a certain order; it follows that it commutes with \(\psi\). One then sets \(f = c\psi\). One remarks in addition that \(\psi\) permutes the \(\theta_i\) among themselves. Indeed, we have \(\psi(\theta_i) > 0\) since \(\theta_i > 0\) and \(c(\psi(\theta_i)) = \psi(c(\theta_i')) < 0\), hence (lemma 5, (c)), \(\psi(\theta_i)\) is equal to some \(\theta_j\). It is now immediate that \(f = c\psi\) answers the question. Indeed, if \(a \in R\), we have \(a = c^m\theta_i\) for some pair \((m,i)\), whence \(a = f^m(\psi^{-m}\theta_i) = f^m\theta_j\) for a certain \(j\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

One can prove that, except in case (2), every orbit of \(f\) has exactly \(h = \mathrm{Card}(R)/n\) elements and contains one and only one \(\theta_i\). In case (2), some of the \(\theta_i\) are superfluous.

footnotes.html: This theorem is due to Chevalley. The writer wishes to express his gratitude to the American Express which, by losing a trunk of Chevalley's manuscripts, obliged him to reconstruct the proof.
For the order relation defined by \((a_1,\ldots,a_n)\).
